Solano County, CA- Rocket Cat Rescue
Ganymede here is a fancy gentleman who is all dressed up with nowhere to go! He came to us with a pretty gnarly eye infection that we managed to get 100% cleared up and now he is a healthy but shy lad who would flourish with more one on one attention.

The male marmalade tabby cat in Breakfast at Tiffany's was named "Orangey Minerva" (1950-1967).

The only animal to win the AHA's PATSY Award twice, he was prized for his ability to stay on set, although he also was infamous for biting actors & handlers (typical star behavior).

our cat loves being a huge cockblocker that if we’re kissing or something he’ll show up and sit on us and be real annoying. but we’ve also figured out we can summon him by just making loud kissing noises at nothing

The cat has decided her preferred position is standing — not sitting, standing — on my chest and shoulders. 

I cannot stress enough that there is over half the couch available to her, including her favorite blanket.

I am feeling deeply favored, but also. This is not comfortable.

Our 21 year old cat, Neko is on monthly vitamin B injections after an illness. He’s kept us up half the night charging around the house, jumping on beams, on our bed and shouting at the top of his voice. 
So he’s doing great, but we are knackered. 🙄

You know what’s cheaper than therapy? A little cat stretching her paw out to touch you. 

You know what’s more mentally damaging than years of trauma? The same cat not wanting to sleep in your lap. 

So really it evens out.

I'm sitting here numb.

After a dramatic cat dad meltdown, we regrouped and I was able to give Raggy the meds he needed. Over a week later and he is doing so well. His ear is healthy and he is no longer twitching or scratching his ear til it bleeds. Happy Raggy. Happy me.

now

I just read the loveliest theory that cats sniff your eyes because they want to check your pheromones (like  in their scent glands) to understand how you’re feeling. 

It’s usually followed by bunting, so definitely affectionate. Ours do it a lot, but only to me.

I can’t stop thinking about it😻💕🥹

🧪 Cat ankles!
Clouded leopards and margays are the only two types of cats that can climb headfirst down trees, owing to the unique flexibility of their ankles (tarsi). 
The tarsi of these arboreal predators rotate around ~180° so that their hind feet can grip even when they are face down/ass up.

So, an interesting property of cats: Uncovering danger

Cats seem to understand that their prior ecological niche was non-apex predator and as such, every interaction they enter, they're constantly reevaluating danger. No matter how cool they are with something, they're prepared for it to all go bad

I just finished reading 11/22/63 and I honestly think it’s one of the best books I’ve ever read. Enthralling, scary and incredibly romantic. It was my first @@3114  book and now I truly can’t wait to read more of his work.

I love paying honor to my Blackness through jokes, stories and the oral tradition.

That said, this a book that I *always* read. It's a compendium of Black folklore going all the way back to Africa. It's always relevant, thought-provoking, and it's the funniest damn book in the world.

Finally, an LGBTQ+ novel with no drugs, sex, or alcohol & hardly even a swear word. It was just a sweet, innocent, coming-of-age story about friendships and first love. The one so many of us have desperately needed for so long.

The Saint of Bright Doors reads like a novel-length short story in the best way possible: full of fresh ideas, fast-moving, with an evident care applied to each and every sentence. Remarkable and highly-recommended.

It’s that time again to share that the best book I’ve read on #grief is ITS OK THAT YOU’RE NOT OK by Megan Devine. It’s unlike any other I’ve read and really has helped me a lot. Highest recommendation for anyone seeking a book from an author who truly gets it because of lived experience.

Diving into this book and wowowowowow is it GREAT from the onset.

I just finished the audiobook of A Psalm for the Wild-Built by Becky Chambers, and I found it comforting in a visceral, bone-deep way that made me almost cry. I often struggle with feeling like I need a purpose or meaning for my life, even though I often disagree with the idea in principle.

Reading Vaults of Vaarn and indie rpg companies keep proving why they deserve more attention than WotC. This book is a condensed, tight rpg with a fascinating setting and incredible tools that you could play in every week until you die all in less than 150 pages. 10/10.

Not only is No Shelter But The Stars gripping and action-packed, it also contains a beautiful romantic arc. These characters, and by extension their cultures, are transformed by a love so perfectly drawn as both powerful and gorgeous. Don't sleep on No Shelter But The Stars, sapphic romance readers!

Discovered this book, Silver Under Nightfall, last night. It's a queer polyamorous vampire book by a nonbinary author. I have extremely high hopes and have ordered it from my local bookstore. Fingers crossed it's good!!

💙📚 2024-7

Winner of the 2023 Booker, deserves all the accolades. I'm not sure I've ever read a book that affected my physically like Paul Lynch's Profit Song. I had to put it down at times. I had to remember to breathe. The anxiety-inducing story of Ireland's fall into fascism is simply brilliant.

Just got my year's tally up to 14 books. 💙📚

12. The Synesthesia Experience by Maureen Seaburg - Insightful non-fiction but felt like an ego trip.

13. Mistborn by Brandon Sanderson - Went well beyond my expectations, wow.

14. Watership Down by Richard Adams - Always a joy to re-read this classic!

Due to my stupid brain and the past year and a half of turbo boosted anxiety, depression and upset, in tandem with having really struggled with reading generally post- pandemic, I only managed to read 21 books this year so I guess that’s something to improve on for 2024.

I am currently reading Anna Smith-Spark's A Sword of Bronze and Ashes. It is a mind-blowing book. I don't think I've ever read anything quite like it. it's a story about the ragged, tearing wound you get when high myth gets stitched to the grind of mundane life. It's incredible.

Pupdate: Woofie went to book club at the Lexington Writer’s Room where he was a Very Good Boy and also my mom came too and so I got to intro both to Silas House, friend, amazing human and writer, who dropped by to talk about his wonderful Lark Ascending #cleareyesfullheartdoginarms

4th book of the year!

My 2nd time for this one. Becky Chambers' work always hits all the right places in all the best ways.

This one is particularly fun. I often describe it to those who aren't familiar as a super queer season of Firefly - and that usually intrigues the right people.

Fanbase Press is honored to announce that our Graphic Medicine series, RIPPLE EFFECTS, has been nominated for a 2023 Harvey Award for Digital Book of the Year! We extend our congrats to the creative team and contributors, as well as to all of the talented nominees!

If you are having a high anxiety week/month/year/life, Courtney Milan's The Marquis Who Mustn't is not only a deeply lovely book, but it can (speaking from experience) push back against near-overwhelming anxiety in a truly remarkable way with its deep faith in people's capacity for good. Highly rec!

Weekly book talk for Saturday, February 4. What’s everyone reading?

If I had the power to compel 330 million Americans to read one and only one book, it would be The Autobiography of Malcolm X (1965).

Came across this cute second hand bookshop in Alsfeld, Germany, the owners run it part-time and they had such a fantastic choice, even a whole Christmas book selection and some quite rare books and antique teapots etc. #writingcommunity

Am reading The Little Rebel, by French author Jérôme Leroy and it is amazing. The story of tensions and terror in a French city, it’s immensely powerful and thought-provoking. It’s only 80 pages and we’ll worth a read.

I've read a lot of Matheson that I haven't liked, but the reputation I AM LEGEND has of being one of the best horror novels, and one of the best science fiction novels, and according to some one of the best novels of any kind, is completely deserved. A once-in-a-lifetime idea, perfectly executed.

finished reading 'Saint Of Bright Doors' by Vajra Chandrasekera. 

You need to read this. One of the best books I've read this year. Chandrasekera created an incredibly messy and meaty world full of terror and religious wonder, with beautiful prose that doesn't let up for the entire book.

I have just finished Bombshell by Sarah MacLean, and it is--to me-- the platonic ideal of a romance novel. Banter! Smexy! A thick thighed hero who's got big Roy Kent vibes! A heroine who's proficient in explosions! I giggled REGULARLY. 

10/5 stars, highest rec.

Finished The Sarah Book by Scott McClanahan. An unpasteurised study of the feral grief of relationship & personal breakdown. Simultaneously one of the funniest & saddest books I have ever read. One of the best too. I'm used to book sadness, book poignancy, book emotions – but this is the real thing.

"Excellent Weird Eco Horror [touching on] Manifest Destiny, familial woes & toxic masculinity. A brilliant piece of fiction. Highly recommended." - Goodreads review of LUMBERJACK, one of The Lineup's Best Indie Horror Books of 2023!

I can't rave about this book enough. I adored it so much. I've loved all of Ethnes's books, but this is my absolute favourite. It blends life's tragedies, triumphs, and absurdities perfectly together. It's also a story about making your own family. Hilarious, laugh out loud and full of heart. #books

hey #horror fans, check this out. The #surreal haunted house that will get under your skin and lay eggs. A dad buries his daughter alive to give her psychic powers. A house falls in love and seduces the mom of the family, driving them all apart. Not to even mention the suicide cult..

This is the kind of fantasy novel that I have been missing. 💙📚

It’s me Austin back again after finishing another @@226765  book & I’m once again asking you read Legendborn so you can then go read Bloodmarked and experience some of the coolest action sequences ever written and the most amazing story about a Black girl reclaiming what’s hers

I was always going to read Rachel Nuwer's MDMA book 
'I feel Love', but it really has exceeded my expectations. It's fascinating and informative, not only on the history and current state of psychedelic science as a whole, but on the nature of trauma. Warmly recommended.

Reading Black Sheep (my second book by @@122762) and I'm starting to realize what I love about her writing. Her protagonists, their voices, it's comforting to see non-male characters who have such rage, and such fear, and so much of it directed in. It feels real. It feels like me.

I am finally reading the Murderbot books after almost everyone I have ever met telling me to do so. And, one book down, they DO NOT disappoint. It reminds me of various 2000Ad strips, in a very good way.

He Who Drowned The World by @@166678 is brilliant, staggering, and hurtful. It, even more than SWBTS, is an incredibly nuanced exploration of gender in an excellent political fantasy. It is brutal, but it has a light shining through. Just fantastic.

Just finished reading OKPsyche by Anya Johanna DeNiro. Cried a few times while reading this book. Piercing and dream-like. Like reading someone's heart.

"a fever-pitched odyssey through the joys, fears, and weirdness of trans adulthood, parenthood, and selfhood in the contemporary world."

Just finished reading Aliette de Bodard's The Red Scholar's Wake, which I very much enjoyed.

If you're looking for lush space opera with pirates and longing and politics and romance, then this is the one for you.

About two weeks until A LIGHT MOST HATEFUL storms into the world ⚡ and I'm delighted to share this blurb from @@25681  whose book RED X is a cornerstone of queer horror. I'm honored by his kind words. Thank you, David 🖤

PINK SLIME by Fernanda Trías (Heather Cleary transl.) is a sombre ecological catastrophe novel. The narrator lives in a coastal city infested with a toxic form of algae. The fish have died, the rich have moved away, and the protagonist suffers through isolation, depression and a family drama.

📚💙 Trying to frantically reach my goodreads goal before the end of the year 😂 6 more books to go until I’ve reached my goal of 50 books! By far the most books I’ve ever read in a year!

I have finished reading the latest @@4966 book, What Feasts at Night, and can tell you that the author is one of the best writers of the experience of having a nightmare that I have ever read. Just, captures the fuckin' WEIRDNESS of bad dreams.

Friends, it's time... for the Avi's Gay Book Choice Awards of 2023! 🌈📚 Here are my top 5 books that came out this year.
-This list is not comprehensive—I read only so many new books this year.
-The books chosen reflect the type of books I like to read and is totally not objective.
Let's get to it!

Today's book rec is Paul Beatty's THE SELLOUT, which possesses in great measure what all powerful satires possess: beneath its comedic madcap zaniness there is a scratching, a tearing; an overwhelming sense of the terror of history bubbling up from below; a profound and wholly justified cosmic rage.

📚 FEVER HOUSE is a diabolic delight of deranged devilry. It's creepy & thrilling with savage stakes and chaos & gore, galore. Keith Rosson has written a terrifying, imaginative story with an occult mythos of mayhem & mischief that takes you on a hellish ride & leaves you wanting more. (Random House)

A person of great taste (who I can’t recall!) gave me this book a while ago and I’ve only just read it. A work of genius that forever changes how you see Jane Eyre. My book reading is going well. Half way through and it doesn’t feel like it. It’s a good feeling. I should get this one done by the end of the month. This will help me stay on my goal of 1 book read a month.

In 1997, Sarabande published LAST CALL, a poetry anthology that became a formative text on the lived experiences of addiction. Now, more than 25 years later, editors Kaveh Akbar and Paige Lewis offer this companion volume for a new generation.

Finished @@166678's HE WHO DROWNED THE WORLD, masterful sequel to SHE WHO BECAME THE SUN. Heartrending, tense, and yet surprisingly hopeful, it asks the question of how far one would go for power and whether it's worth it.

Four of my fav this year. Scotty Morrison’s book is one I dip in and out of all the time. The Danish one was a bit of a plod at times but its important to me cos I am trying to learn more about Denmark and my heritage. Emmas book a must read. And the grief one is just so beautiful.

Finished reading The Bloody Chamber by Angela Carter last night. It was fantastic! Thanks to the kind soul who helped me sort out a digital copy. Any fans of this book? Any favorite stories? Loved the takes on Bluebeard and Puss in Boots

Book 1 of 2024 is Different Seasons by Stephen King 

I had never read any Stephen King before this and I was simply blown away. Four very different stories that affected me in so many different ways. Reading him is like when a scissors starts to glide through wrapping paper. Effortless.

Absolutely blown away by C. J. Leede's MAEVE FLY, which sets itself higher and higher hurdles and then backflips over every one of them. It invites so many comparisons veteran authors would quake at, but it earns them. Mean, nasty, solipsistic, amazing.

I almost got through the year without reading any Muriel Spark which is unlike me so I’ve put that right and am having a wonderful time reading The Ballad of Peckham Rye on audio… it’s full of quirky characters and is set so local to me I can picture the scenes vividly! 💙📚

Amal El-Mohtar is wonderfully generous to THE SAINT OF BRIGHT DOORS in the New York Times: “the best book I’ve read all year. Protean, singular, original … I can’t remember the last time a book made me so excited about its existence, its casual challenge to what a fantasy novel could be.”

My third book of the year was Redshirts, by John Scalzi. It's a classic parody, but also critique of writers and the people who make money on them, an existential tome of philosophy, and an examination of interpersonal relationships.

Lunchtime reading! I'm the generation to whom Calvin & Hobbes meant everything, so I was excited by the Mysteries' existence and relieved when I read it. Waterson could have become anything in 28 years. This gorgeous serenely nihilistic fable for adults shows the irritable moral compass remains true

reading Manhunt this evening by Gretchen Felker-Martin, & one thing that strikes me is how beautiful everything is. The way she describes the settings, the world, the fog, the mist, the burned out buildings. There are tender moments, & beautiful, & when combined w the violence & terror it's dizzying

Still buzzing at how much I enjoyed 'Anyone But You'. An absolutely terrific romcom. Also shows how basically if you just cleave to the principles of a Nora Ephron romance you'll probably make a great romcom. And if you cleave to those principles while riffing off Shakespeare, you're cooking.

Finished THE SAINT OF BRIGHT DOORS by @@64002; what a tremendous reading experience. Do NOT let this one fade from your sight, friends. 

Buy a copy, and experience a story that was by turns tender & Weird, surprising & horrific, beautiful in all angles. WHEW. I gotta lie down.

Book 20 in 2023 ✅

This book made me feel warm and it made me feel like I was being hugged the whole time. The characters were phenomenally written out and the writing style was just so beautiful. A wonderful story about love and belonging that will stay with me for a long time. Highly recommend. 🩵

requisite naked goblin legendary speaker orating in the middle of my drinking hall double fisting massive tomes

Maggie Nelson’s memoir, The Argonauts, encompassing her transitioning partner and meditations on queer motherhood is maybe one of the best things I’ve read. I’ll be teaching it next semester in the second half of my queer memoir course

Welp with 2 hours to spare I hit my minimum goal of averaging reading a book every two weeks this year. Wanted to do more, as the screenshots show, but I read some good stuff this year. Take all these as recommendations, and a look inside my intellectual life.

Do you like sex and nerd shit? 🥵 Look no further! This book is two-in-one, tricking you into reading theory by sandwiching it in erotica. ✨

Fall for my mad genius plan by pre-ordering it! 👩‍🔬 In a bookstore near you February 13th. 📚

Glitterati, by Oliver K Langmead!

Dystopian hellscape but make it ✨fashion✨

Like America's Next Top Model meets Hunger Games meets capitalist fashion nightmare.

You will most certainly hate EVERYONE in this book. But, my darling, you will LOVE every second.

@@54775

Just finished Lucifer by Dan Watters, Max Fiumara, Sebastian Fiumara et al.

Heads up: Goodreads giveaway for @@4129 upcoming haunting thriller The Drowning House. What’s more ominous than an entire house washing ashore?

Today's book is A Memory Called Empire by Arkady Martine. This book is so, so, so good. Lots of people have read it, but lots more should.

This morning I finished reading Catriona Ward's LOOKING GLASS SOUND, and it was an absolute wonder of a book.

Here's the cover of our forthcoming Recovered Books Boiler House Press edition of Macdonald Harris's Mortal Leap, out in March 2024. And here's what Wes Minter of Seattle's Third Place Books once wrote about it: "A book so far beyond 'good' that its greatness can only be experienced."

More than two months since the release of the Far Reaches collection and the individual stories are still rocking the bestseller charts on Amazon: Six for six at the top of the science fiction category, in fact. That's pretty cool.

“Since The Good Companions was published over thirty years ago it has defied the cheap sneers of some highbrows with more than forty English editions and impressions alone.”

Tremendous blurb on this 1962 Penguin Modern Classics edition of J.B. Priestley’s 1929 novel.

Book 1 of 2024 is done. Dexter & Sinister Detecting Agents by Keith W. Dickinson.

A fun steampunk murder mystery with a talking cat. Can you ask for more? Well, okay, Dexter is not a 'real' cat, as he is quick to point out, but that just makes him more sarcastic.

Easily one of the best books you’ll read this year❗️

I’m currently re-reading this. 

I first read it in the spring, when tbh my head was reeling. The last chapter made me sob. I had to come back to it. 

It’s a first person account of what it’s like to discover you’re trans. Very readable, and also good for any cis folk who’d like to understand more.

Read T. Kingfisher's What Feasts at Night on the plane, and loved it so, so much. Reminds me a little of Sheri Tepper's Marianne trilogy (my favorite of Tepper's many books/series) in terms of setting & spookiness.

Excellent book. Posits the rise of ISIS not as a genuine upheaval of devout religiosity but rather as another strand of right-wing populism that attacks the intellectual class and offers a sense of action, however deeply evil, in a liberal world that seems paralyzed by inaction.

I’ve spent the evening with Essex Hemphill’s 1992 poetry and prose collection, Ceremonies. Dealing with black masculinity and queerness, the AIDS epidemic, kink and sex, and essays on the problem of Robert Mapplethorpe and the joy of Paris is Burning. Highly recommend

Having slept on it I'm confident saying that The Malazan Book of the Fallen is the best fantasy series I have ever read. Completely engrossed me from start to finish. Found a wonderful architecture bookstore in Amsterdam, simply called Architectura & Natural Boeken. The urban planning books were completely different than what you’d seen in an American bookstore. It’s like we’re having a completely different conversation than the rest of the world.

This book was an excellent buy. It behaves like a book when approached (cannot vouch for the times when no observer is present), looks like a book when seen in 3 dimensional space time, and feels like a book in the hand. If a book is what you're after, this is a book.

I just reread Jeanne Thornton's Summer Fun because I am teaching it. We exhaust our superlatives so easily online, and i have few left that are not devalued, but I have not read many more beautiful novels in my life and if you have not read it you should

Starling House by Alix E. Harrow.

A gorgeous gothic novel with a haunted house at its center and metatextual footnotes. I love how this story explores how history is created and our understanding of the past, how the stories we tell become what's true.

Not sure why we missed Last Tango in Halifax on the beeb the first time but making up for it now. It is very good. Even go far as to say soothing… and very well written as to be expected by Sally Wainwright.

Out next week from Penguin! I was delighted to read this ahead of publication - it's a really exciting, fascinating and well-written book about travel, trade, mobility, connections, encounters, hostilities, & imagination in the late medieval world. A must-read!

The Tower of Life is celebrating its 1st bday! Susan Gal and I want to express our deepest gratitude to everyone who has embraced this book--editor Dianne Hess, Team Yaffa at Scholastic, our families, agents, reviewers, booksellers, awards committees, & our readers! Light, laughter, love!

Finished Solenoid by Mircea Cartarescu, tr Sean Cotter. A battle at times, but worth the effort I think. A richly imaginative stream/waterslide of consciousness slipping between dream states and reality for a nobody Romanian school teacher. Lots of great lines and tough paragraphs.

Wonderful Sunday reading: this Mexican novel, a fragmentary non-chronological Bildungsroman based on Yukio Mishima’s travels to Mexico and the US as well as his formation as a writer. One of the best Mexican books I’ve read recently. I hope it makes it to translation

Publication day for our Boiler House Press Recovered Books reissue of Gertrude Trevelyan's powerful psychological novel William's Wife. Trevelyan's work is finally getting the recognition it deserves.

Now granted there aren’t *that* many trans sapphic books out there, and I certainly haven’t read them all, but what strikes me is how DIFFERENT they all are in vibe, mouthfeel, and approach, and how amazing it would be if there were 👏 many 👏 hundreds 👏 more 👏

A reminder that that collection comes out from Cursed Morsels Press in Summer 2024. Queer, bloody, Latine, and, yes, very ACAB-y.

A bit late to the party on this one - but absolutely loved Drunk on All Your Strange New Words. Compulsive reading - I've found myself sneaking chapters in between marking papers! Firm recommend for a near-future mystery about translation and the value of truth.

I read 227 books this year. I had set a goal of 200, but when I had an unexpected 4 month break in my school (thanks, ruptured brain aneurysm!) I had lots and lots of time to read and ended up surpassing my goal. Many were romance, with some detective/mystery and speculative fiction on the side.

This sapphic romance was a good listen. I've never read a book set in Ottawa, or in the world of competitive Scottish Highlands dance. Moira and Kenzie are 20-yr-old life-long dance rivals that are brought together during a college scholarship process. Gotta ❤️ an enemies-to-lovers story! 
💙📚 🌈📚 🌈💜

First book read in the new year is "Hi Honey, I'm Homo" by @@792 

If you're familiar with Matt's youtube videos (and you should be) this is the same well-researched, well laid-out, incisive history of the intersection between the gay rights and media representation. Recommended.

I just finished "The Violence" by @@17559 & I can say it's one of the best books I've ever read.
It's an intense emotional rollercoaster with very tragic, sweet & happy moments. All too real scenario about human abysses & the hard fight against them.
I smiled, I shed tears. 5/5 ⭐

The Lion has been voted as Black Library's book of the year for 2023! Thanks to everyone who voted: I'm so happy that it apparently caught the imagination of so many people.

And also that I finished above Dan Abnett in a list, that's not something I thought would ever happen.

Well, just powered to the end of Fiona McFarlane’s *The Sun Walks Down*. One of the more exceptional novels I've read in a while. Beautiful writing and genuinely acute insight into characters … and also into the Australian desert landscapes … in a tense, missing child plot. Major recommendation.

I began my week with this wondrous book, a needed balm when the world hurts and I’m drowning in work. It is a masterful novella that packs a sophisticated and deeply affecting reflection on the historical layers of loss in a text of perfectly distilled economy. A most deserved Nobel.

Light From Uncommon Stars 	by @@117814

This book deliver a young transgender virtuoso and deals with demons. Oh, and space aliens running a donut shop in California.

And so much gay.

I loved this book so much. I highly recommend!

Today's book rec is Emil Ferris' MY FAVORITE THING IS MONSTERS. Her haunting work grapples with childhood, family trauma, the political crises of the 1960s, and the legacy of the Holocaust, drawn in a style that feels like a fresh amalgam of Robert Crumb, pulp horror, Lynda Barry, and George Grosz.

Brilliant short crime stories by @@177147 

Gritty but with much heart. In just a handful of pages characters come alive with depth and a rich inner life, each story left me hungry for more. Somewhere between Fargo (also due to the location) and Franzen.

I find so much pleasure in reading Helene Hanff that I just don't want her books to end. I bought this unassuming little number from Kennys Bookshop in Galway, and it has spent some time in Limerick. It smells like a book nerd's idea of heaven, and is perfect in every way.

ok i finished @@64002’s the saint of bright doors and it’s one of those rare books that the best kind of recommendation i can give is… just that you must read it. for me to talk about how it made me feel would rob you of something special. an incredible read, and on so many levels.

Murder On The Orient Express. 

My first Agatha Christie read and of course, it won't be my last. I liked the interview format and the twists and turns. Things change up before it felt too repetitive. 

A good reminder of how little French I remember 😌 

Looking forward to the movie(s) next 💙📚👀

Patrick Kyle’s THE DEATH OF THE MASTER (Koyama, 2019) is a pleasure—a striking panoramic view of a community failing and flailing to uphold the ideology that defines it, with wonderful cartooning whose putty-like malleability belies a wonderfully tactile literalism.

Finished Farthings by Charles Boyle – 113 short pieces (a para each) about life as a one man publishing house CBEditions. I enjoyed it enormously. Like Alan Bennett's diaries only with a sharper edge. Miraculously free of industry bitterness, but lots of wry insights about how publishing works.

I finished Some Desperate Glory by @@535729 & it really is spectacular. it's military SF that upsets all the conventions of that genre, it's a moral meditation on how one can be both oppressed & oppressor, & somehow it's also about family & friendship. I can't recommend it enough.

Book #15 of 2024: JESUS’ SON by Denis Johnson, a book I reread once a year. “All these weirdos, and me getting a little better every day right in the midst of them. I had never known, never even imagined for a heartbeat, that there might be a place for people like us.”

The black-headed gull is a small gull that breeds in much of the Palearctic including Europe and also in coastal eastern Canada. Most of the population is migratory and winters further south, but some birds reside in the milder westernmost areas of Europe

🧪The Andean cock-of-the-rock, a clear example of sexual dimorphism! Males have a large crest and vibrant orange coloration, while females are more subdued in their brown plumage. Interestingly, even their iris color is sexually dimorphic, with only females exhibiting that striking blue 👀

The Gold-ringed Tanager has the local common name Bangsia de Tatama for the very limited range where it is found. The tanager is the symbol of Tatama National Park and a species that has inspired conservation in Colombia. 🪶🌿

Open water on the lakes and ponds around Anchorage #Alaska is getting pretty scarce but Common Goldeneyes can get by with surprisingly little water. A few usually manage to stick it out after most other ducks have bailed on us. 🪶

The pink cockatoo, also known as Major Mitchell's cockatoo or Leadbeater's cockatoo, is a medium-sized cockatoo that inhabits arid and semi-arid inland areas across Australia, except the northeast.

Pacific Golden Plover seen on the Kona Coast of Hawaii. 

Pacific Golden Plovers breed in the summer months in Siberia and Alaska, and migrate south to Asia, Australasia, and Pacific islands for much of the winter. The Hawaiian population is well adapted to humans and are common park birds.

Black Phoebe. These birds have a long range - from the western US south all the way to Argentina. They are primarily insectivorous. If you love birds, gotta conserve bugs. 🌿🧪🪶

Bald Eagle 🦅 

The Bald Eagle embodies grace, power, and adaptability, revered across cultures as a symbol of resilience and connection to the natural world.

They can fly at 20-40 mph but reach up to mind-blowing 100 mph while diving.

Probably the most majestic of them all!
#birds 🪶

More good news! We've found a record number of the scarce Brown Hairstreak #butterfly (Thecla betulae) eggs at Rock Edge SSSI. Forty nine eggs around the site, including 2 triplets and 2 doubles. Only 3 eggs on Blackthorn, 36 on Cherry Plum and 10 on another Prunus species...
#UKWildlife #OX3

A Sparrowhawk is probably one of the most exciting birds you might see in a garden. Thanks to John Lauper for sharing his sighting.

Female Sparrowhawks are dark brown in colour and larger than the males, who have slate-grey backs.

Parenting of swallows. The swallow's parent bird flies at great speed and shoves a large dragonfly into the chick's mouth. The chicks also try their best to drink the dragonfly. It's powerful.
#birds🪶

We spotted this handsome Barred Fruiteater skulking around and at first thought it was a thrush. These birds are true to their name and only eat fruit. They can be found in the Andes Mountains from Venezuela south to Bolivia. Tatamà National Park, Colombia 🧪🌿🪶

When I see a hawk perching, it's usually very far away and 80 to 100 feet up.  This red-shouldered hawk plopped down on a branch that was less than 15 feet up and relatively close.  Huntley Meadows Park, Alexandria, Virginia, USA 30NOV2023. #birds

Though I know it’s the most colorful, flashy birds that usually draw us into birding, I’ve come to be more interested in the subtleties that distinguish diverse but sometimes overlooked groups, like sparrows. Here, though soon to be renamed, a juvenile Harris’ Sparrow in Bixby, OK USA. 🪶

This handsome little scooter is a Cinnamon Flycatcher. They call the Andean cloud forest home and can be found from Venezuela south to Chile. Like most flycatchers you can see them sallying from a perch to catch flying insects. Montezuma Rainforest Lodge, Colombia 🪶🧪🌿

A dull day travelling along the Niagara Parkway next to the Niagara River (Canada-side) brightened instantly by a gaggle of at least 55 delightful migrating trumpeter swans swimming along almost single file & engaged in their customary honking. 🪶#birds #waterfowl

Bird Glamour loves Halloween colored birds! The Baltimore Oriole loves ripe bright-colored fruit, especially oranges. They also eat many insects we consider pests, like tent caterpillars. Female orioles weave elaborate hanging sock-shaped nests and will recycle old nest materials. 🐡🧪 #BirdGlamour

🧪Meet the Steller's sea eagle (Haliaeetus pelagicus), among the largest of all eagle species by both weight and wingspan! This individual is one half of a mated pair being cared for by Woodland Park Zoo as part of one of the U.S.’s few conservation breeding programs for this species 🦅

BARN SWALLOW ATTACK! 🪶

Record year of rain meant we got vernal pools on the bluff! 

The buffet of bugs attracted the largest mixed flock of swallows I’ve ever seen at our park

It gave many opportunities to miss focus on swallows. And a few lucky hits!

Golden Tanagers are native to the cloud forests of South America from Venezuela to Bolivia. They eat a combination of fruit and insects and tend to travel in mixed flocks. 
Bella Vista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador 🧪🌿🪶

The level of fluffiness of this anhinga resting on a tree is amazing. As their feather coat is not waterproof (like ducks), I saw most of them sunbathing with spread wings soaked in water.

But this one was already air-dried in the warm sun of Costa Rica 
🪶

🪶🦉Rose-ringed parakeet, enjoying some winter sunshine in an Antwerp park, where they established a feral colony in the last decade. They seem to perform assembly flights above the city at dusk, before roosting together. 
(halsbandparkiet, Psittacula krameri)

The great blue heron is a large wading bird in the heron family Ardeidae, common near the shores of open water and in wetlands over most of North and Central America, as well as far northwestern South America, the Caribbean and the Galápagos Islands

A pair of Trumpeter Swans nests (dangerously close) to a road ~10 minutes from my house; I usually drive by and check on the cygnets every couple days. These little fluffballs become the largest native waterfowl in North America in no-time flat - the adults are 6+ feet long and weigh over 25 lbs 🪶

The kingfisher that lives by my place finally agreed to let me photograph her! Just after this she caught a fish nearly a third her size and proceeded to whack it against the branch until it was dead.

Belted Kingfisher - San Francisco, CA 🪶

The eared grebe has a piercingly red eye — totally freaky. In the fall almost all of them fly to Mono Lake or Great Salt Lake and pig out on brine shrimp and alkali flies, losing most of their flight ability and doubling in weight. They migrate at night. #Birds

It has a pinkish-brown body, striking black and white wings, a black downcurved bill, and a pinkish-brown crest which it raises when excited.
   The Eurasian hoopoe is native to Europe, Asia and the northern half of Africa.

Outside of Kazakhstan: Finally we could watch the migration of the Eurasian crane in Kelbra, Germany. It was fantastic to see thousands of birds arriving in dusk at this lake to fly later further to Spain. These birds also living in Kazakhstan and migrating in winter to India.

The flame bowerbird is one of the most brilliantly colored bowerbirds. The male is a medium-sized bird, up to 25 cm long, with flame orange and golden yellow plumage, elongated neck plumes, and a yellow-tipped black tail. It builds an "avenue-type" bower with two side walls of sticks.

A visit to Merritt Island National Wildlife Refuge is quickly becoming a yearly ritual. The photos don't really do it justice. The amount of wildlife (mostly birds and alligators) is tremendous and utterly amazing. 

A few highlights attached. 🪶

American flamingos are one of the largest species of flamingo. Found in the Caribbean and along the northern coast of South America, these extraordinarily social birds live in flocks that sometimes number in the thousands. A pigment in their food gives their feathers their flamboyant pink color.

Pale-billed Flowerpecker seen on the IIT Bombay campus, Mumbai, Maharashtra, India.

They're hard birds to get a good look at: Tiny (only 8 cm long), and they spend most of their time foraging for fruits and flowers (often from epiphytes and mistletoes) in the tree canopy. 

#birds

There are some species at that are on the top 10 most wanted birds of many searchers and the Diademed sandpiper-plover is one of those. What a thrill when I finally caught up with this species high in the Andes of #Peru a decade ago. 🪶

I got to see this very photogenic lifer yesterday: a brilliant white Lark Sparrow that was hanging out in a flock of finches and bluebirds, foraging among the dew-covered grass at a local campground. 🪶

My fav gull! 90% of them nest on Isla Rasa in the Gulf of California but a small population began nesting on an island in a small lake and then on the roof of a McDonalds in Seaside, CA near Monterey. The island eroded & McD's burned down, but some friends of mine built a new island for them!

A drake Common Goldeneye stretching his wings at @@299642 Saltholme reserve yesterday. Small numbers of them are wintering on the East Saltholme Pool and usually a few may linger until second half of April. #ukbirding #ukwildlife 🪶

New bowerbird paper! Male great bowerbirds build display courts that are linked to mating success, and we found that object availability 🧱 rather than male building ability 👷 explains variation in court quality rb.gy/vznmm7

The final Bird Glamour Glamager look is the Flame-colored Tanager! This complex-colored tanager calls Mexico & Central America home & sometimes AZ home. They are a mountain bird, shifting their territory to higher elevations due to climate change, but mountains only go so high! 🐡🧪

4 bald eagles (1 adult & 3 juvies, perhaps the triplets born last year on the lake) trying to hunt a cormorant or kleptoparasitize it, aka harassing it until it gives up fish that it caught - a strategy often used by eagles since they are big enough to bully other animals to steal food! #SciComm 🧪🦑🦈

Mt Laguna is also home to one of my all-time favorite birds: the Acorn Woodpecker! This time they were busy gorging themselves on the abundant insects flying over Little Laguna Lake, unseasonably filled by the tropical storm 🪶

"In a spectacular sight, flocks of various species of water birds migrating from different parts of Eurasia have started arriving at Point Calimere Wildlife and Bird Sanctuary in Nagapattinam district."

Endangered California gnatcatcher in the wonderful coastal sage habitat here down by the Mexican border in the Tijuana River Valley open space preserve. In the first shot he is doing his mewing call that sounds like a kitten. 🪶

Last week our lone blue-jay learned that mimicking a hawk will cause every squirrel at the feeder to dive for cover.

Blue Dashers - one of the most common dragonflies I see throughout the season - they perch on a favorite spot, get chased by another Blue Dasher, circle around to chase the one that took his place - the females have sporty stripes and are not as easily seen while they oviposit #Invertebrates 🌿

A fantastic photo to start your week. Taken by my friend Alan McFadyen, who runs local wildlife photography hides. He took the famous pic of a kingfisher diving vertically, just before it entered the water.

First snake eagle of the year just seen banking over the hills beyond the motorway outside of Ioannina. The fingers on its massive wings riffling with wind; its pale feathers ghostly against the slanted sunlight. Travel well, beautiful bird.

Bird Glamour’s Glamager series continues with Hepatic Tanagers! This southern US to Central American dark red bird loves a pine-oak forest. These birds are little studied, but we do know they eat insects, spiders, and small fruits. Lady Hepatic Tanagers do most of the nest building. 🐡

Still going thru my Alaska pics-- here;s a nice Red Phalarope Female in her bright breeding plumage. Utqiagvik (Barrow) Alaska, the Northhern-most city in North America is a great place to see shorebirds up close. #Birds

“The left side of its body was covered in shimmering spring-green feathers, the classic coloring for females. Its right side, however, was iridescent blue, the telltale marker of a male. The bird appeared to be a bilateral gynandromorph: female on one side and male on the other..."

Snow petrels live in the most extreme environments. They breed amongst boulders high on Antarctic nunataks where summer temperatures may be -15 Celsius. To find food they have to fly hundreds of kilometres back out to the edge of the sea ice

🌍 🧪🪶🦑

This past year was wild! We had storms that brought some mountain bluebirds to nearly sea level for about 3 weeks in the spring.

For context, the park I frequent is 2 miles from the Pacific Ocean. The bluff overlooking the park where this bird was found is no higher than 100 feet in elevation! 🪶

🦉🪶 A couple of weeks ago,  I had the pleasure of watching several kingfishers speardive for a few hours... WHAT A SPECTACLE ! Even without the extraordinary colors of its plumage, anything but a "common" kingfisher. (Ijsvogel, Alcedo atthis)

Greater Yellowlegs and its little buddy Lesser Yellowlegs giving a nice rare comparison this evening at Steigerwald Lake in  Washougal, Washington. Greater is not interested in sharing its salamander. #birding

How to survive a winter storm: sit on feet, maximize floof, hunker down, take a nap.

Maybe this Mourning Dove is dreaming of warmer weather - finding a nice partner dove and throwing a few sticks together to make a nest. 🪶

Gray Catbird numbers are way down from last week. Most of the pokeweed, Virginia creeper, and wild grape vines have been pretty well stripped of their fruits. I suspect this may be one of the last ones I see until next spring. Safe travels! #birds #fall #migration 🪶

Female Red-winged Blackbirds are acrobatic insect hunters! 

A few years ago I happened upon one dancing on pond muck - I was entranced. Now I go look for them and feel joy at seeing and hearing their little contact calls to fledglings on the shore. 🪶🌿

Black Phoebes used to be rare to infrequent in Oregon, especially the Northern parts, but now are more and more common. This one graced our presence at Eagle Lookout at Ankeny National Wildlife Refuge today in Marion County, Oregon. #birding

Wading birds are so fascinating and interesting to watch. Of all of them, I have to say this one is my favorite. #birds #nature

Tricolored Heron

Masked Trogons are common throughout the Andes cloud forests. These birds eat fruit and they also catch flying insects. This male was busy looking around for a meal.
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador 🪶🌿🧪

Collared Incas are snazzy little hummingbirds. Most of the time when you see them, they look like they are wearing a black tuxedo. But if you catch them in just the right light, you can see some green to their feathers. 🧪🌿🪶
Tatama National Park, Colombia

Canada jays are quintessential northern birds - omnivorous, with thick fluffy plumage. They don't migrate, and breed in late winter (Feb/March) before arrival of spring migrants to their boreal forest home 🪶

Blue-winged Mountain Tanagers are common, widespread tanagers found in the Andean cloud forest. Their favorites food are insects, fruits, and small berries. And yes, this bird is a stunner. It is unmistakable with its bright yellow, black, and electric blue coloring. 🪶

Magnificent shot and one of my favorite birds that I am lucky enough to see quite often here in San Diego.  Looks like you’ve got a youngster.  I don’t think I’ve ever seen a more graceful bird in flight except for the swallow tailed kite. 🦉🌿

Ladder-backed Woodpeckers (Dryobates scalaris) are native to the SW US and MX. They can be inconspicuous and quiet, requiring a bit of time and patience to find. Their small size and agility make them deft foragers.

Woodpecker Wednesday! 🪶🌿

Flies, fabulous flies. Estimates vary, but there are about 1 million fly species in the world and only around 14% of them have been described. Here are few gems from my little patch. 🧪

Black Scavenger Fly (Sepsis sp.), Marsh Fly (Dicta sp.) and Tachinid Fly.

The Common Kingfisher has to be one of the UK's most beautiful birds: it looks like it belongs in the tropics rather than fishing along a stream in a wee London park next to a children's playground, but that's where I found this handsome chap this morning in Colliers Wood. 🪶

A beautiful Red-tailed Hawk I had the opportunity of seeing today while at work. They flew low at first and then perched on this branch for 30 minutes or so. I see this species decently often while I'm driving perched on power lines but I almost never have the chance to photograph them. 🪶

A Great Blue Heron from the Anchorage Coastal Wildlife Refuge #Alaska (last month). An everyday bird for many of you, well those in North America, but we are at the very edge of the range and lucky to get a sighting a year.

A stunning Scarlet-Bellied Mountain Tanager working on some feather care - the bird was happily bathing. These fruit eating birds can be found in the high forests of the Andes Mountains from Venezuela to Bolivia. 🌿🧪🪶🐡
Zuroloma Birding, Nono, Ecuador

Last year we drove down to San Juan Capistrano to see the swallows and when we got there we were informed that the swallows no longer live at the mission and if we wanted to see them they now live underneath an overpass.

On the Hunt - Northern Water Shrew 🦋 
The North American Water Shrew is our smallest diving mammal at only 14-16cm long. It is primarily nocturnal, hunting in the pitch black of rivers and ponds by using its long whiskers to feel and blowing air bubbles which it then inhales to smell for prey.

The pyrrhuloxia or desert cardinal is a bird that inhabits the arid region from the southwestern United States to central Mexico.
It is similar to the northern cardinal in terms of song and behavior, and both coexist in many desert areas.

Gang-gang Cockatoos! These endangered beauties are roughly the size of Galahs and limited to a small area of coastal South-Eastern Australia. The local flock visited our garden yesterday, and I counted *sixteen* of them!

The great white pelican also known as the eastern white pelican, rosy pelican or simply white pelican is a bird in the pelican family. 
            It breeds from southeastern Europe through Asia and Africa, in swamps and shallow lakes.

We have been hosting gracklepalooza at our place as more than a hundred had gathered in preparation for their journey south. Most have moved on but we still have quite a few. This male common grackle was foraging in the trees  and really showing off the colours!

Bird Glamour’s Glamager series continues with the striking Scarlet Tanager! This eastern North America & South American tanager are surprisingly difficult to spot: they prefer to hang out high in the forest canopy. They also do a wardrobe change in the fall until spring to a yellow-green plumage! 🐡

If it weren’t for the watchful eye of my travel buddy while we were inside the restaurant eating, we likely would missed these two beauties (male & female). It was our one and only sighting from the trip! #birds

Long-tailed Silky-flycatcher
November 25, 2023
Savegre Valley, Costa Rica

The Kolea are back! After flying for 8000 miles or so down from the arctic, an entire flock (about 50 or so) arrived at Kealia Pond NWR last week. Pacific golden plovers are amazing....this one still showing some breeding plumage. :) 🦉📷🌿

🦅📉 Arizona's bald eagle population was reduced to three pairs in 1970! 😮‍💨

Due to elimination of DDT, habitat protection, & artificial reproduction efforts there were 69 breeding pairs, & a record 87 young bald eagles hatched during 2019. 📈

#ArtAdventCalendar Day 5: Bald Eagle, Lynx Lake, AZ 🪶🌿🐡

The jetties at Playa del Rey are the best place in Los Angeles to see surfbirds, including this one starting to molt into its summer plumage yesterday. In a few months, they will depart for Alaska and the Yukon 🪶📷

Wonky winter wildlife! For some reason this Gray Catbird decided to stay on the north shore of Lake Ontario this winter. It was chowing down on invasive European Buckthorn berries which are aplenty so I guess it's doing well enough. 🪶

If you love birds, you need to conserve bugs. In this BugShot - Birding crossover event we have a Lemon-browed Flycatcher with a katydid. The bird say there with it so it was probably waiting for us to leave so it could head to its nest. 🌿🪶#inverts

The 🪶 Purple Gallinule calls are ubiquitous in Tortuguero National Park, Costa Rica, but it's visual presence is elusive, often calling from deep in the floating grasses it calls home

📷📸

#wildlife #photography

For many of you a common bird but White-throated Sparrows are quite rare in #Alaska. This one has been spending the winter in Anchorage. Same spot as there was one last winter but I didn't get a picture to compare. 🪶

Oystercatcher searching for food in rocks exposed during low tide along shoreline of San Francisco Bay in Emeryville, California.  Early sundown for first photo and sun has almost set in second. (I saw an Oystercatcher years ago and it took another year or so to see another.)
#Birds 🦉
#Photography 📷

What a day! Visited Last Mountain Lake Migratory Bird Sanctuary (Saskatchewan, Canada) to spend a day watching the greatest show on earth. Saw about 30 bird species, and easily 50,000 individuals. Geese, ducks, cranes, harriers, eagles... 🪶

A Grey Catbird I saw at MacGregor Point PP in Ontario.

Catbirds like to skulk around in the bushes so it was a treat to see one in the relative open. They are related to thrashers and mockingbirds, so as well as their namesake mewing, they have a vast repertoire of elaborate vocalizations.

Just going thru the pics on my phone: i used to have this spot where I could go watch a pair of eagles raise their young every Feb to June. 4 seasons I got to see it. They loved these rotten sycamores for some reason. They nested in them until they just couldn’t anymore- and moved on. 2-19-2021 🪶

I spent a small amount of time watching pacific ladder-backed — I mean… Nuttall’s woodpeckers… mating

After he finishes, he stays a while and strokes the back of her head a few times before they leave 

Such an intimate behavior to witness 🥰🪶

A rainbow lorikeet about to stick its head in a tree. 🪶

Absolutely stunning bird; lovely call.  It always astounds me how something like this can so completely disappear after landing in a tree.

Canon R6mkii + RF100-400mm

Honing photography skills picking off wary warblers certainly helps with flighty rainforest birds. This handsome friend is a Common Tody-Flycatcher and it never stopped moving! 
Montezume Rainforest Lodge, Colombia
🧪🌿🪶

"The wings that escape sound" 

The Windbane Naga is a giant flying sebecid and the Flagship of Eyes of Tiamat. It does almost everything in the sky, only landing in its roost to rest. Its speed and ability are unmatched, said to dart across mountains.

We’ve had king tides + storm surge… waves were dangerously large and the birds prefer the safety and calmness of the bay.

This was one of two brown pelicans doing short dives fishing in the channel near my work 🪶

Quite rare around Anchorage #Alaska, I was surprised to meet the Double-crested Cormorant while kayaking earlier this fall. I guess it was surprised to see me too as it declined to stay on the same lake as me.

we've had a lot of activity from one of my favorite birds, the black-footed albatross, who mainly nests along the leeward islands of Hawai'i & forages hundreds of miles of the North Pacific for food to feed their chicks

🌊🦑🪶🌿📷🌎

🪶🦉Almost as beautiful,  the little egret isn't really that much smaller than yesterday's great egret. Especially if you keep in mind the cattle egret, which is chicken-sized. Getting more common in Belgium every year. Lovely. (Kleine zilverreiger, Egretta garzetta)

This is a plover chick. Plovers lay their eggs in the middle of open spaces on the ground and attack anyone nearing their nests. But they are are wonderful birds just trying to be good parents. Please be kind to Plovers. I am a Plover lover and you should be too.

Great Blue Heron taking a 17-minute bath in a lagoon next to San Francisco Bay, in four parts. 
1. He floats lazily about. 
2. He sits down in water. 
3. He dives in and shakes himself off. 
4. He flies away, probably to nearby pine tree.
#Birds 🪶
#Photography📷

I spotted a heron wading in the river on my bike ride home. Hard to see in this picture but it's on the rock at the top of the photo

I've always considered it lucky to see a heron

But then it started raining on the ride home, so maybe this wasn't a very lucky heron

I was delighted to find my wife her lifer Clapper Rail two weeks ago (at Huntington Beach State Park in South Carolina, USA)

Gotta admit, though, this individual is pretty rumbled-looking, rough lifer for a species that is often sleek!

We were happy to find a pair of Powerful Woodpeckers in Ecuador, where they aren't very common. If you wonder what an Ivory-billed Woodpecker looked like in the wild, the Powerful Woodpecker is in the same genus and looks similar.
Female (L), Male (R) 
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

🧪One of the coolest critters I’ve ever seen, an aptly named rhinoceros katydid (Copiphora rhinoceros) spotted in the Monteverde area of Costa Rica. I’ve never been much of an entomology gal, but this guy sold me 🦗

Swallow-tailed Cotinga in the Serra do Mar outside Rio de Janeiro last month - a #bird I hadn't expected to see as bad weather forced me to cancel a planned day of higher altitude birding, so a wonderful surprise when this one turned outside the lodge as I waited out rainstorms. 🪶 #BirdsOfBrazil

Most woodpeckers are black and white. This is true in South America too, but the Crimson Mantled Woodpecker breaks that trend. They are considered common, but this summer was the first time I saw one. 🪶🌿🧪
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

Learning the ropes as a young Red-tailed Hawk is challenging - annual survival to one year is about 36% - this youngster is likely looking at a gray squirrel (which is tricky prey) running up a tree & hiding - a bit baffled, the hawk circled the tree & then looked off into the distance 🪶🌿

Fall migration had started here in Minnesota, bringing with it nighthawks in need of help. Living most of their lives on the wing, nighthawks even drink while flying, swooping down over water to grab a sip. It makes them very difficult patients since they don't readily eat or drink without aid.

#ArtAdventCalendar 2023
Dec.19
Yellow -rumped warblers are among the first warblers to arrive back after their winter vacation. 
This male had just arrived and is flying down to the creek for a drink.🪶

Some underwater critters from Saturday out at Tāwharanui. You'll see spotties, snapper, goat fish, sweep, hiwihiwi and a few eagle rays, one feeding. Also, a lot of attempts to capture the beautiful colours of the seaweed, the ground and the sponges. #MarineLife 🦑 #Kikorangi #Aotearoa

Hopalong is back! My one legged blackbird, not seen for months, reappeared in the garden this morning in his usual eating place, no longer molting, a full array of feathers. Now fat and full in his usual perch. Definitely him, same markings on his tail and by his left eye. This makes me so happy!

Bird Glamour’s Glamager celebrates the Summer Tanager! This southern US to Central American bright red bird is both beautiful and deadly. Summer Tanagers specialize in eating bees and wasps, which they catch mid-flight and then beat to death by whacking their catch against a branch! 🐦 🐡

While looking for squirrel pictures I was reminded of one of my favorite warbler photos of all time. Audubon's Yellow-rumped Warbler in a tangle of dead and weathered branches. I found this little guy at Lassen National Park. The Audubon's form is mostly found in mountainous western North America. 🪶

A snazzy Golden-naped Tanager pausing for a photo. These birds came be found in the Andean cloud forest from Colombia to Bolivia. They are omnivorous and dine on fruit and insects. 🌿🧪🪶
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

Hello Bluesky. Here's the 'Type Specimen' art for my favourite bird, the Yellow-throated Scrubwren, from my soon to be published 'Little Brown Birds of the Sydney Region'. This bird has a fascinating, feeding and breeding ecology with many exciting mutualisms and commensalisms. #birds #birding

Nine cockatoos turned up to eat berries so I went out to get a pic. Did they fly away? No they did not. Four of them flew over to the rail near me, stared a bit and started preening. Or snogging. Very shy.

Welcome to the #BirdGlamour Glamager series, starting with the Western Tanager from western North America! The Western Tanager is extra and DOES NOT get his red coloration from a diet rich in carotenoids like many birds do: their red comes from rhodoxanthin from their insect-rich diet. 🐡🐦

Sunset lady in the park, feeding squirrels dinner, leaves before the gates are locked, taking jackdaws with her, who pewpew from her garden as the fox slinks in, to eat the meat she roasted hoping it will fatten him.
She watches from her kitchen, knitting squares for unknown kin.

#whistpr #mpotd

Western meadowlark in front of a great egret over a vernal pool! 

Earlier this year, we had record rain in Southern California. Our local park has  vernal pools with fairy shrimp! 

An abundant water year made it possible for the scene to happen as western meadowlark’s winter in on our bluff￼ 🪶

Mama Mallard with her baby ducklings all in a row, with the exception of two off on a little adventure. The last duckling is holding spots for their siblings. Blue water of lagoon next to San Francisco Bay.
#Birds 🪶
#Photography 📷

Best bird photos from 2023.
When word got out that rare scarlet-chested parrots had been seen in big numbers at Gluepot, birders (including me) flocked to see them. I had searched for these birds for years. This is a female scarlet-chested parrot feeding on cottony saltbush.
🪶 #birds

It's a bank holiday in Spain, so I had time for a proper walk  this morning & spotted the most cooperative kingfisher ever. I followed it from rock to rock along the sea front for more than 10 minutes. 1/3 #birds

A Little Blue Heron and some White Ibises doing some afternoon fishing.

Ibis Fact: White Ibis nests can have nests from ground to as high up as 50ft 

L.B. Heron Fact: These Herons have white feathers that transition to its blue color over the first year of their lives/

#FloridaWildlife #Birds

Sitting on the porch watching flocks of 20 or so tiny birds scavenging the garden. Not sure if they are sparrows or finches but they are enjoying all of the waning garden plants including a few coneflowers which are persevering 🌱

Great egrets: masters of the art of striking a pose. With a slow, sassy strut, they wade through the waters in pursuit of their aquatic prey. Showcasing their prowess as ambush predators, these birds are striking in more than one way! Nature’s choreography unfolds in these feathered maestros.

Here's a great blue heron (I believe?) taking off from a pond in suburban Kansas City. The kids around the pond were in awe of the it flying away! Good reminder that urban wildlife is so important for connecting kids with their environment. 

🦉

This bird chowing down on a peanut is a brown thrasher. I suspect it may be the same one from last year, since they normally prefer thickets and shy away from houses. This one prefers the marsh and backyards.
📷📸🪶

A great part of Sayulita, Mexico: there's one patch of the Pacific Ocean that apparently is filled with the dumbest fish. Over and over brown pelicans speared into the water, shooting out their heads at the last moment like a snake striking to snatch fish from the water. #Mexico #Birds #Birding

Two nēnēs at WWT Slimbridge! Native to Hawai'i, and the rarest goose in the world, at one point numbering only 30 birds due to hunting and introduced predators like mongoose, pigs, and cats. Decades of conservation efforts have helped reintroduce the nēnē and native numbers are over 3,800 today 😊🪶

One of the exciting things about this time of year is the return of the nuthatches! They are in our  area all year, but we don’t see them nearly as often in the summer. This red-breasted Nuthatch was foraging on the tree trunks near sunset!🪶

Took a bunch of photos of Osprey foraging over the saltmarsh at Huntington Beach State Park (South Carolina, USA) over Thanksgiving Break (November 2023)

Assumed they were epic shots, only to learn after-the-fact I'd bumped my camera settings so there was still a little bit of blur:

One of my favorite photos from Kaua'i was of this Apapane feeding in an ohia tree, the signature Hawaiian native forest tree and main food source for these honeycreepers. Apapane are endemic to Hawaii and are found on most of the larger islands 🪶📷

Cassin’s Finch brings #BirdGlamour to conifer & quaking aspen forests in western North America! Similar to the House Finch but with a pointier crown, Cassin’s Finches are excellent mimics and add other species’ songs to theirs. They’re also a bit salty: they love salt and visit mineral deposits. 🐡🧪🐦

THE LILAC-BREASTFED ROLLER
 
Is a beautifully colored African bird from the roller family, famous for its acrobatic aerial displays during the breeding season. It is a Native of Southern & Eastern Africa, It prefers open woodland and savanna,& it is usually absent from treeless places.#Blacksky.

Waxwing - Bombycilla garrulus

After 90 minutes waiting for them at Woodford Lodge, the call came this one was spotted by the main road/entrance junction.

The under-appreciated Palm Tanager! They are not as flashy as the other guys but they are just as gorgeous in their own right. This could be male or female as there is no sexual dimorphism with this species. 

Palm Tanager 
(Thraupis palmarum)

Few wildlife spectacles epitomise the brutality of the natural world quite like the sight of a black-headed gull, carrying a croissant the size of its head, being chased by eight herring gulls over Morrison’s car park.

As a first post I'll share this amazing image from my bike commute. This is something you can only see biking or walking to work over the Longhorn Dam on Pleasant Valley. IRL there are herons fishing.

You might have seen my "Manny" the Mandarin stuff...

From that time we took our grandson to see bald eagles and feed the ducks out at Lynx Lake...and he called me over to 'check out the two weird ducks" he found....🤯

TWO Mandarin drakes...in Arizona. 🪶🌿

Manny wasn't shy, so he got all the love!

Superb owl photo my wife took in 2016 in Brian Piccolo Park near Fort Lauderdale. The park is full of nesting burrowing owls who have no problem living among the people using the park for recreation, it’s really an amazing thing to see.

I grew up in a pretty competitive birding environment, mostly friendly but occasionally a little catty

I just love the Champaign Illinois birding crew in comparison. Folks helping each other find and ID birds, crediting others by name in rare eBird reports, so pleasant

Birding level reached: 🪶 Resplendent Quetzal 
This bird, a member of the trogon family, is one of the most extravagant natural users of metamaterials. Backlit, its feathers appear a dull gray, but when light reflects, they light up, traversing blue-green-golden depending on the angle. #wildlife

This is not the most exciting bird in the world, and it most likely will not make your day, but this is a Belding’s Savannah Sparrow, an endangered resident darling of the estuary, found only in salt marshes. 🪶🌿😎

At the moment I live in Germany in area called “Bird’s Mountain” of which a big part ist nature reserve. It does not take long to see birds of pray but it needs some portion of luck to capture this little guy 🪶(kingfisher) even if it’s so common here that it became logo of regional beer 
#birds

Today's #101CuriousTales concerns the Woodpigeon, one of Europe's most numerous #birds. Such high numbers of a farmland bird makes these a major pest species. In the UK Woodpigeons are resident, but here & elsewhere in Europe huge numbers will soon be moving south.  1/N 🧪🌎🌿

🔊👂 Southern ground hornbills are quite vocal! These social birds can inflate their brightly-colored throat pouches to let out loud, booming calls. This is done to announce location or attract a mate.

📷: Zookeeper Taylor S

Great Blue Heron in tree, looking down at lagoon with other Herons, Egrets and occasional Canada Geese.  Next to San Francisco Bay, across from Trader Vic's.  Happy New Year, all!   🎉🪅🥳

GaryAnne (large female Cooper’s hawk) just stopped by as we’re having lunch on the patio. She flew down to a water bowl for a few sips and to cool her jets, err, feet 😁
Interesting, she ‘kneels’ while drinking. 
Not the same cooper’s that hangs out in the olive tree.

The Bald Eagles at the local park will sometimes take sticks from the Great Blue Heron rookery and add them to their nest. This Bald Eagle picked up a stick, but the stick must not have met the Eagle's quality standards because it was dropped a few moments after this photo was taken.

Willow ptarmigan are such fun birds. Like many northern species, they moult into a nearly all-white plumage for winter to blend into their surroundings. However, they still stick out a bit when standing on crushed ice. 🪶🌿

In May 2022 4 Glossy Ibis arrived on the Hampshire coast. They were mobile at first eventually preferring floods along the River Meon. In March, right image, they were still in winter plumage but by April they’d attained full summer. They moved on in summer but are back again now. #birds 🪶#ukbirding

I saw this eagle flying over Big Marsh Park this morning. An immature bald eagle, maybe about one and a half years old, I guess? I wondered if this might be a golden eagle, but the Merlin and iNaturalist apps think it's a bald eagle.

Warning: R-rated content. Avert your eyes! 😄
Good to see the resident pied oystercatchers at Bald Hill Beach working on their family planning. They have been so unlucky. Every year they try. Every year they fail to become parents. Maybe this time?
🪶 #birds

At dusk, a Great White Egret is stretching its wings and about to take flight from a lagoon that is right next to San Francisco Bay. The water is aerated fresh water mixed with salt water from the Bay which enters when the tide is rising. A variety of birds often feed here.
#Birds 🪶
#Photography 📷

this bird doesn’t look like much but you need to know that she is shown here taking a short layover on her 7000 mile journey from her breeding ground at the arctic circle to central South America where she spends her winters so she really is a lot

Broad-billed motmot with a quite water-soaked feather dress.

Bet he was as surprised as me when the sudden rain shower started 🌧️

Spotting it was pure luck: Motmots usually wait motionless on branches until potential prey moves (insects).

Semi-stormy morning/lunch gave stunning views of a young Pomarine Skua hunting at the beach. Surprisingly, when all was calm(ish), ≥25 Eur + 1 Leach's #Storm-petrels were still around—in beautiful evening sunlight! Some new local #birds and "my" #eBird hotspot's broken 100 species & turned green. 😊

Many think of Marbled Godwits as prairie birds (well coastal in winter) but there are also breeding populations on the James Bay lowlands and on the Alaska Peninsula. This one is from the #Alaska population during a stopover on the Anchor River State Recreation Area 🪶

The red-crested cardinal (Paroaria coronata) is a passerine bird in the tanager family Thraupidae. Notwithstanding its similar name, this bird is not closely related to the true cardinal family Cardinalidae. It is sometimes known as the Brazilian cardinal.

#Photography #Birds

Barnegat part 11B: Harlequin Ducks edition continued. At Barnegat, these ducks like to hang out on the inlet side of the jetty, fairly close to the jetty walls. When they’ve shown up locally, they tend to hang out by our breakwalls along the beach, where Lake Erie tends to be the roughest. 🪶

Frigatebirds have tiny little legs and feet. They can't land on water because they are unable to run for a takeoff roll like waterfowl. So what is a thirsty, dirty frigatebird to do? Crash and fly, my friends. Amazing to see this.
Floreana Island, Galapagos, 2018

Another year gone, and our thoughts turn to which were the best jackdaws of 2023. The third best jackdaws were the ones at Harlow Carr. The second best were the ones near our house. The best were the jackdaws at Beamish (pictured). Well done to them, and commiserations to all the other jackdaws.

Ridgway’s rail! 🪶

This endangered species is easily heard in parts of the Bay Area, but very hard to see as they skulk in marsh grass. At Arrowhead Marsh, I’ve seen one just twice in 15 trips—fleetingly, at a distance. 

But yesterday, because of the very high tide, long, clear sightings of FOUR.

Everyone stop what you're doing and look at my baby bird!
 
See the tiny head poking out?
The last couple of weeks have been so exciting watching Mumma bird working so hard for this little one
This is about a foot from my window and I can see them sitting up in bed
I love this little bird family

Hundreds of canvasback ducks in the Niagara River yesterday. Was able to count four separate paddlings but nowhere to pull over along the Niagara Parkway for closer pics. Here are two of the four mass gatherings we came across, always an amazing sight to see even on a very dull (& difficult) day. 🪶

Steller’s jay 🪶

📷 UC Berkeley Botanical Garden 

(It’s been surprisingly difficult to get a good pic of these very common, very loud birds. They’re dark, and tend to hang out high in similarly dark canopies.)

Despite living in the so-called “Prairie State” I don’t have a lot of access to prairies 

Little Weaver Park in Urbana is a precious sliver of prairie, full of grassland sparrows and a rumored bobcat.

There’s a proposal to turn it into sports fields. Just utterly depressing.

These pink Black-headed Gulls are seen every spring at less than 1 in a 1000 frequency. This one is an extreme example. It is male and if you look at all the pics, you'll see the pinkness extends to the undertail coverts.

Photos © Barry Yates

god i love watching a reddish egret just launch itself through the water, wings out, head swinging to and fro. It's a hilarious method of hunting AND IT WORKS. these dudes rustle up a ton of food running around like a maniac in the water.

Dramatic pose!

Ruby Throated Hummingbird migration still passing through the Florida Panhandle. Most of the males are long gone by now, but I'm still seeing the females, like this beautiful girl backlit by the morning sun.

For some reference...they close off the entire eastern half of Lynx Lake from hikers to protect the breeding pair of bald eagles. I see most of your smarter types getting in kayaks to get over to them for clicks. 🛶

I shot across the whole lake! 🤣

Cedar Waxwing
 
They are elegant-looking birds named for beads of shiny red material on the tips of the secondary wing feathers.
      It breeds in northern forests of Eurasia and America and every few years irrupts far southward in winter.

Everyone knows the skin is the best part.

Juvenile Red-tailed hawk with prey (I think a scrub jay but it took the head off before I could positively ID). San Diego, CA. #birds 🪶🦅#photography📸#sciart🐡

My best lifer of 2023 was a woodpecker, and ironically, my best self-find was also one. I was thrilled to find a family of Red-headed Woodpeckers within a few km of our camp, a very uncommon bird for that area #birds

Here's a female Great-tailed Grackle. A "garbage" bird of San Antonio, but I love them. They are loud, flamboyant, and gather in great numbers all over town. This one was enjoying a fountain at the Pearl in downtown. 🪶

We went to an area of Lake Springfield where a Limpkin had been repeatedly sighted. No Limpkin but lots of Ring-billed Gulls. The 2nd pic provides an example of trying to walk with dignity while being windblown.🪶

This female #pileated #woodpecker seems to be daydreaming while clinging to a thin branch.  She really seemed to enjoy the blueberry-like fruit on this tree.  My arborist-fu is weak, so if anyone can tell me what type of tree that is, I would be grateful. Burke, Virginia, USA 13NOV2023 #birds

A Blue-winged Mountain Tanager enjoys some fresh fruit. These birds are common and widespread in the Andean cloud forest. I believe the plant is a Clusia sp. Birds provide an important service to the forest by spreading fruit seeds. 
🧪🌿🪶
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

Landing gear down!
Red-necked stints (like these at Bald Hill Beach) are our smallest migratory shorebirds.
They breed in Siberia and Alaska during our winter so rack up a lot of frequent flyer points.
🪶 #birds

Thought I'd get a good pic this time of trumpeter swans in the Niagara River close to river's edge. But alas, as soon as I approached them, even though slowly, they began to swim away. Was still a treat to see them.😊#birds #waterfowl

Our Halloween-colored #BirdGlamour celebration continues with the Hooded Oriole! Seen in southwest US and Mexico, they’re also known as palm leaf orioles because the sew their sack nests on the undersides of palm fronds. Hooded Orioles are chatterboxes and will mimic woodpeckers and flycatchers!🧪🐡🐦

A park ranger in Santee SP in SC once pointed out to me that the round nesting holes in the trees in the middle of Lake Marion are from Pileated Woodpeckers, but the large oval ones were made decades ago by Ivory Billed Woodpeckers, and this fact honestly haunts me - all that's left is a void. 🪶

Down on the flood plain in search of a rare visitor to these lakes. A morning of mud-splatter and cracked ice; of snipe flashing off the wet meadows and grey partridges thundering from thickets. And then there it was, the sudden immensity of a white-tailed sea eagle watching the winter waters.

The terror of Montezuma Rainforest Lodge. This little Rufous-tailed Hummingbird liked to hide in the bushes an attempt to chase off any other hummingbird in his territory.
Tatamà National Park, Colombia 🧪🌿🪶

The Atlantic puffin, also known as the common puffin, is a species of seabird in the auk family. It is the only puffin native to the Atlantic Ocean; two related species, the tufted puffin and the horned puffin are found in the northeastern Pacific.

Let's pretend it's July 23rd instead of January 23rd. Please enjoy this Black Skimmer skimming! They zoom along the surface of the water and catch fish and look so elegant doing it. 

#birds 🌿 #marinelife

Lunchtime in the backyard today on this slightly blustery kind of sunny day in San Diego north county. 
1st - mostly Anna’s hummingbirds, I think
