My peppers and eggplants sprouted!!! This gives me so much joy. I had to get a grow tent so these would sprout in the basement (they need 70+ degree temps to germinate). My tomatoes germed last year w/just heat mat, but got stunted at 2 inches. Ambient heat just wasn't enough. Hopefully tent helps

The official start of this year's gardening.
Not much to look at just yet.
Spent an hour moving some rhubarb roots to make a permanent space for my potted fruit trees.
Then cut down the autumn rapberry canes and moved pots of spring flowering bulbs to that location to give them a boost of sunshine

This cherry tomato plant is wild as far as size vs production. When I first bought it and it was 1/5 of this size I had to pull a ton of tiny tomatoes off it just to get the plant this big. Favorite thing in my garden this year 🌱

🌱The tomato seeds* I planted 8 days ago are coming up like gang busters.

Today I'm buying a new LED shop light (4800° - 5500 lumens) as the daylight coming through the windows is just too weak.

I've been away from home for three weeks. Came back to find a teeny seedling I left in the hydroponic planter has suddenly taken off. This was grown from a 20-year-old seed packet I found when we moved last year. Only one of the seeds germinated, but it's determined to make up for the rest.

A rescued rose, well, sort of. The plant has had powdery mildew really badly and a regular spray of a hydrogen peroxide solution (1 part H2O2 to 3 parts water) has knocked that way back. The bloom is much paler and subtler in appearance than my phone camera can reproduce. 🌱

We've had so much rain here, my tomato plants are my height. This is a photo at eye level of my tomato patch. Spent the afternoon pruning them down so air flows in between them. Now if the tomatoes would only ripen up... 🌱🍅🍅

Cosmos is the flower for the birth month of October and symbolizes balance and harmony.It attracts butterflies and is easily used as an attractive border or backdrop plant.Cosmos is simple and easy to grow from seed sown in the spring, readily reseeds itself,and tolerates a wide range of soil types.

Carrots struggle to grow at my place because I am too keen a soil feeder for there liking. But planted these after kūmara last year so in a sandy mix that was a bit depleted and look. Herself was good enough to pose with it

🌱 Saying goodbye to the last of the flowering gladioli. First time growing them this year and I adore them. Just need to give them better support next year. Thinking of planting some dwarf ones given the wind we get here.

A little Sunday trivia for ya:

I love Trillium 🤍 seeing them means spring is coming. It’s also Wisconsin’s State Flower. Don’t pick them because doing so can disrupt the plant’s ability to sustain itself and it can take years to bloom again- once established they can live 100 years!

My favorites among the lilies. Those white ones are, like, 5 plants total. They put out a whole bouquet of blossoms per stem which is beautiful but impractical. The final image is my absolute favorite of the varieties we grew this year. Just stunning 🌱

🌱 Rolling the dice in the garden. Im seeing volunteer sweet peas in other parts of the garden so this week i sowed some sweet pea seeds in a raised bed with a trellis. Will have to wait and see what happens next. Either way, it’ll teach me a lesson😊.

My Christmas amaryllis that usually blooms for my birthday in Jan is finally blooming. I ordered from a new supplier and they came late and then molded so I ended up with a replacement even later. I guess it’s my St. Patricks Day amaryllis this year.🌱 It’s gorgeous though. Almost worth the wait.

Nights are getting cold. Lettuce plants have gone to seed. The summer-salad harvest has ended. 🌱

This was our first year growing them, got a lot of fresh salads. I’m curious to see what romaine & red lettuce flowers look like. 🥬 🌼

(Sure I can Google it. Don’t wanna. I’d rather just wait & see. 🤗)

Singleaf piñon, Pinus monophylla, is the only single-needle pine - all others have 2-5 needles in bundles. It has large seeds, piñones, that are delicious. Piñones are important to Native Americans throughout the  range of the tree. This is an old tree on the side of a canyon near Petrified Forest.

I feel like lying down and dying but that’s not allowed so instead I planted more things: eggplant, zucchini, chillies, snow peas, capsicum. In a couple of days I’ll do tomatoes and maybe some sunflowers

A pretty seedling we have never named or released but we refer to as 'Biddy's Pink' on account of the recently deceased Biddy Barrett telling us most determinedly that it should be named. Huge flower but too close to 'Iolanthe'.

I absolutely love a wild garden. My tomatoes (cherry here) have decided no container can hold them. 

Late start for everything this year (except lettuce) due to really heavy rains but it's about to explode now.

🌱

Plants update: Got two pumpkins growing on each of the long vines, one of which has started climbing a nearby tree, two squash babies have just popped up which is exciting, and I have about 16 tomatoes between the two tomato plants, no two are the same size

Planted a random mix packet of sweet peas in a container and let the climb my (ugly) metal fence. Wasn’t sure if I’d see bloom because it heated up so fast here, but look! First sweet pea blossom!!! I’m so proud. 🌱

Gardening update!🌱

🥒 The zucchini plants are flowering! Must've happened overnight--I saw no orange/flowering at all yesterday!!
 
🍒 The cherry tree is getting close to harvest!

🍎 The apple tree has TONS of apples! Will they grow larger than this? :P

🌶 Peppers are growing! Beautiful deep green!

I’m in zone 8b and it’s almost January but look at my alyssum! Everything else has given up the ghost but it is absolutely thriving!

The appearance of the apricot flowers is more elegant than that of the plums, and the color balance between the white petals and the red calyxes is pleasant 🌸

Just hand pollinated this beauty in the 1st pic. The 2nd, 3rd, & 4th pic show the progression a pollinated flower will take. The fruit in the 4th pic will continue to ripen & eventually turn purple & then wrinkle, then it will be at its sweetest. So yummy. 🥰

Today's Gardening Advice: Magnolia virginiana, or Sweetbay Magnolia, is an excellent native small tree/shrub.  It produces creamy white, heavily scented flowers throughout summer.  May retain some foliage in winter.  Tolerates moist soil.  Tends to be multi-stemmed. 🌱

Velvet-leaf mallows are going in for a fall-time bloom! I saw this super tall one (it was taller than me!) in BEAUTIFUL bloom on the N Salado Creek Greenway while on a run this afternoon. 

Velvet-leaf mallows are easily one of my favorite native wildflowers. They bloom from May through November! 🌿

I'm not much of a flower person, but we got several kinds for the 91 yo FIL to sprout and he's having a blast. Not sure where we are going to PLANT the 250 chrysanthemums he's currently sprouting, but it is the sprouting bit he finds exciting, so maybe it doesn't matter. 🏵️🪻🌼

Shire Giant Sunflower, for all of you delightful sunflower fans!
These easy to grow giants reach up to 12' tall. The heads grow up to 14" across & weigh 6 lbs! A favorite of bees & birds alike, they also make excellent living poles for climbing beans. 🌱💚🌻
Sunflowers symbolize loyalty & adoration.

Sliced one of my purple tomatoes and it's so pretty. 🌱 Flavour is nice. Not intense or anything but a little tart, a little sweet, nice texture. Also, my body is now 80% tomato. (and yes, saving some seeds!)

Going to be planting some roses this spring, mostly yellow, maybe some white/pink, preferably fragrant. I like the Peace Rose a lot, and found a nice variant called Chicago Peace. Any other gardeners have favorite roses? (My zone is 6A.)

I’m a hobby gardener. At the beginning of the pandemic I panic planted a load of potatoes on the advice of my Irish hubs (who swears his dad grew their annual family supply on an allotment in Dublin using basically no effort). In the fall, I harvested the entire patch (yeah that’s a dime):

This really doesn’t look much but I am so excited - the colchium emerges! First time growing one and I was worried I planted too late and had seen others in full bloom so thought it might not do anything but no! Here it is! 🌱

🌱 Harvested the last of the grapes from one vine. This was given to me by one of my neighbors 2y ago but first time fruiting. Very distinct fruity taste unlike the grapes in the grocery store. Also much smaller.

Last year my coffee tree got his first 5 or 6 blossoms. Since then it's been a long journey. It took so many months to get 1 red coffee cherry. More of them will follow. Obviously not enough to roast. How many beans do I need for a brew? 😂
I think I'd better plant them.

We picked our first tomato today! True black brandywine, just over 17oz. It’s about halfway ripe, so we still need to wait a bit before eating it. (There is another from the same plant, of similar size, that we are letting ripen further on the vine as a point of comparison)

Drying and curing finished.

Found 4 seeds in here so far. I'm aware that the plant was developing a few male flowers, but I guess I didn't catch them all. Looks like the plant successfully self-pollenated.

How does it look?

Some wildflowers need a period of freezing temps in order to sprout in spring. We plant ours in fall and let nature take care of the rest. Our Rocket Larkspur is a little confused by the warm weather! 🌱💚 #gardening

Cécile Brunner, the Sweetheart Rose, small but perfectly formed, like tiny Tea Roses, in abundance throughout summer. The scent is light, but nevertheless it makes the perfect buttonhole rose - which is one reason why i had to grow it. Buttonholes for the boss.

🌱 Perennial hollyhocks (Alcalthaea suffrutescens "Parkallee & "Parkfrieden") in the morning light of the autumn equinox. They bloom from July until October (sometimes longer) and are very popular with wild bees.

my stepdad dad just gave me NINE port albert cucumber seedlings. i'm rich!!!! ⬇️ these buggers if you don't know. delicious but very hit & miss when you're trying to germinate seeds, none of mine took. anyway they are the fresh taste of summer and i've heard we're gonna actually have one this year so

8/8 Macintosh apples are doing well. 2/4 Johnny Appleseed Apple are doing well. The are just growing at their own pace. Transplant time soon! As I have a mix, I will get hybrids with these. 👍

Adelaide d'Orleans - a Sempervirens hybrid, dainty, hanging sprays of bloom - so elegant and with a myrrh scent. (1826, Jacques, France). I needed absolutely no encouragement or special reason to grow this one - her sheer beauty was quite enough.

My wife loves freshly squeezed lemon in her water every morning, so this year we're attempting to grow them. Don't know if we get enough sun but we're trying. Very excited to see progress, and really hope there's enough summer left to bear fruit!

Had to finally move these Fuchsias into the greenhouse yesterday as I knew the overnight temperatures would plummet.  They’ve flowered better recently than they did during summer.

drying & collecting tomato seeds is very simple. u just take them out of the fruit, put them on a plate & leave them on the side for a day or so to dry. after that u scrape them off with a blunt knife or a fingernail, & tip them into lil envelopes & u have seeds ready for the next few years!

Every time I see one of these, I kick myself for not having planted one.

Callicarpa bodinieri really deserves its name of beautyberry. I saw this on a trip out to a Cotswold garden today. 

I just need to find space in my own garden. 

#GardeningFeed

My heath aster is blooming! I’m so excited about this stuff. I planted 3-4 babies last fall and it has spread beautifully. Keeps down weeds, flowers in fall when the pollinators need food, and is native to much of N America. Seems hardy af so far. Why don’t I see this everywhere as ground cover? 🌱

As the hellebores are finishing, lungwort is getting started.  The lungwort is a volunteer seedling from Pulmonria ‘Trevi Fountain’. The hellebore is a white-flowered hybrid whose sepals turn jade green after the petals have fallen. 🌱

Throwback to my first mini garden. I planted with no knowledge and ended up with mostly diseased crops 💀 I learned so much from trial/error alone but do some research before throwing seeds around! I'm excited to take what I have learned by ACTUALLY studying and make something magical this year. 🌱

Good morning. My geraniums not only survived 18 days without watering, they thrived. Look at these pretties we came back to. I hope everyone with geraniums potted some up for winter enjoyment in a sunny location indoors.

This is the perfect season to grow Lemon Balm in Florida! Your plant will be flourishing with leaves, which should be harvested often. As the intense heat of Summer arrives, the plant will wilt and possibly die back. Cut it back and in the Fall it will likely come back to life! 

📷 Tanria Elementals

I planted this up last year and I'm so proud. It was sulky to begin with but now the roots have grown through the spout compost to reach the water reservoir and is now growing like the clappers in the middle of winter.

Today's garden job was transplanting lupins from my nursery bed into the borders. These are so easy to grow from seed as a short-lived perennial and they self-seed everywhere on my #allotment. Much more resistant to slugs than plants from the garden centre! 😊🌱

I found some purple Texas fall blooms while on my 13.1 yesterday! Purple is my favorite color, therefore purple wildflowers are top tier. Always feel free to tag me in any purple wildflower posts! I’d love to see them! 🌿

I noticed that Turk’s caps along the Salado Creek are going in for a fall-time bloom. They flower from late spring through late fall/early winter here. A good number of them flowered around June/July this year but the recent drought conditions kept them at bay for a bit. Glad to see them again! 🌿

Garlic is one of the easiest crops to grow. The cloves of a bulb are split and planted with the point upwards. These can be started indoors in pots or directly planted outside… 🌱

just learned that cotton is a mallow (i've never seen it in flower, just yoinked standing dry stalks off the roadside in oklahoma) and now understand why the weird prickly seed heads of hollyhocks are vaguely familiar... plants are so cool dude

I'm overwintering some Thai pepper plants and they are still trying their hardest to grow! My babies need to chill out and rest!

4/6 tomato seedlings look happy but the other two look sad. I bought a mixture of colours I'll be so sad if they're all the same. In other news I am working through clearing my raised bed for summer salads. I neglected it post covid because spoons and LO! I have some heckin lorge beetroot!

Torrential rain here today so I spent it in the greenhouse. Sowed thyme, basil, brussels sprouts, peas, tomatoes, aubergines, broccoli and agastache. I also pricked out lettuce, sunflowers and heleniums. Busy busy! 🌱😊

DRAMA! I just went out to talk to my veggie garden. The cucumbers were STRANGLING the tomato plants!! The cucumbers have taken over my garden and wrapped their tendrils around  the neighbouring plants. NO SIRS. You back off.

My first poblanos are starting to fruit! Now if only we get some sun instead of rain...

This first year of trying to grow hot peppers in Alberta has been fun and exciting and I've learned a lot, but I also would like to have lots of peppers on my plants please and thank you. 😂🌱

🌱 Considering what they've been through & most of the tray contents were on the floor 😱 happy to see any of these Pansy seedlings up. Germinated at odd angles & in clumps due to their early shakeup 🙈 so spaced them out a bit. Will leave as-is until planting out next month.

my pepper plants spent all summer in these crappy little pots because I was too lazy to plant them. now that it's cold I brought them inside but they're still producing. powerful determination from these little guys. 🌱

Unbelievable these tomatoes 🍅 were still growing in the veg patch a week ago, however, as green as apples. After a week in a paper bag in the cupboard under the stairs, they are now perfectly ripe and ready to enjoy.

She’s not all the way open yet, but this Wannabe dahlia makes me especially happy.

Today's harvest from the garden. This is definitely our best Courgette year. It just keeps going and going. And this is like 8x as many bean pods as we got last season. And there's still more and more flowers

Omg omg okay so I transferred all the lil ladies into nursery pots and I got them all trays so no leaking! Peep the empty tray on the bottom right, THATS FOR STOCK BABIE!!!!!! Hogie’s House Plants is gonna be a real life thing!

I am going to brag a little. Poast? This is alocasia melo. Rare, and very finicky. I moved it from Maryland to NY, and it is thriving. It gets a heating pad under the rocks, and lots of humidity but not much water. 🥰

More in plants gave me moments of joy today: the fall sedum is dripping in pollinators, my domino cactus is pushing a bloom, and one of our infrequently mowed, weeded for invasive, but otherwise untouched areas is filled with hoary aster blooms.

Tulips & Daffodils
have always had a particular allure for me at the beginning of March, meant to banish my personal SAD-addled blues, and prepare for brighter days, longer daylight, and the promise of Summer’s warmth

Feed your eyes with color and your mood will follow

Today's Gardening Advice: Certain wildflowers deserve to stay right in the woods.  Various orchids and trillium are difficult to establish in the average garden.  Orchids like the common lady slipper have symbiotic relationships with fungus.  Gardeners are too often tempted to try them and fail. 🌱

Many daisies in Aotearoa (New Zealand) are shrubs or small trees. Heketara (Olearia rani) is flowering now. This is a cluster of tiny florets: 12 outer white ray florets each have a bilobed stigma; 5 inner yellow disk florets, each with 5 stamens joined in a tube & a stigma that pushes out pollen.

And the first pumpkin flower! Weirdly this is a female flowering, when I think it’s usually the male flowers that show up first? Not sure this one will end up pollinated.

This variety is ‘Cinderella’, should be big and orange like a pumpkin carriage! 🌱

So many exciting things going on in the garden now that spring is on its way! Our kiwi berries have survived the winter and are growing new foliage, the bluebells have arrived, and tulips are starting to show. 🌱 #GardeningFeed #FlowerReport

Scarlet and Dazzling Blue Kale are just gorgeous now. Transplanted half to greenhouse beds a month or so ago. The other half remained indoors, getting more light, and are much more "scarlet." Going to pick some this weekend. 🌱

Lousy summer here in the west of Scotland, but a much better crop than last year of outdoor tomatoes despite all that. Unfortunately I lost the names at the planting out stage but a mixture of Gardener's Delight, Mountain Magic (my favourite, grows well here) and ...um... a beefsteaky type. 🌱

the tiniest green shoots spring out on what I thought was a dead lemon verbena plant as bees buzz loudly all around. hard pic to take but a reminder that when you think there's nothing left to salvage there might be new life 🌱

The creek disappears into a dark tree-cave at the back of my property. Last year while clearing nearby brush I went in, shocked to find pieces of hydrangeas clinging to the sandy, pebbled banks. Their stems/roots were mostly petrified driftwood. I relocated 5. 4 have recovered. 2 have bloomed! 🌱 🌿

The Tamarisk trees are also in full flower right now. Quite a special tree, being drought and salt tolerant. I know it is invasive in some countries but at home here in Spain. 
I only noticed the heavily pollen laden bee in the background afterwards. 🐝🌱🦉

Today's Gardening Advice: Hemerocallis "Katahdin" is one of my favorite daylilies. Tall scapes, prolific flowers, and grows well. A Ron Valente introduction, it is cold hardy and named for a mountain in Maine. Not the most fancy flower, but a robust, dependable performer. 🌱

Nasturtiums are a must for any garden. Beautiful, unusual, and fully edible. The bees also go nuts for them.

Not sold? They also love poor soil and will help fix it, great companion plants to deter pests, and perfect cut flowers.

🌺 nasturtiums 🌸 flowers 🧑🏼‍🌾 gardening 🌱 plants 🥗 harvest 💦 irrigation

For fellow gardeners surrounded/ shaded out by black walnut trees 🌱: I sowed 3 tomato plants (sun gold) in my backyard, mere feet from the canopy edges of the dreaded juglone zone. Absolutely unaffected. Further context: my death-strip is the south canopy edges where squirrels drop the most husks.

As we wait for spring to officially spring here in Nashville, the cherry tree I planted in my front yard is giving a glorious preview. I was out of town for this show last year so I can’t stop staring at it. Obsessed! 🌱

Hibiscus plants are renowned for their vibrant and showy flowers, making them a beloved addition to gardens and landscapes. Beyond their ornamental value, they have cultural, medicinal, and culinary significance, contributing to their diverse appeal and usefulness in various parts of the world.

This week marks the start of my tomato harvest, featuring hearty Romas, radiant Sungolds, and classic cherry tomatoes. My foray into organic gardening was a labour of love, using seaweed mulch, mushroom compost, etc. If the weather had been a tad warmer, this would have been a bumper crop!

When I miscarried, I buried my embryo in a houseplant. I didn't know what else to do. As part of my ongoing healing, I clone the hibiscus every time she needs a trim. Most have not survived. One is very healthy. These are her very first clones.  She is ready for a bigger pot.🌱

The winter rains turned my yard of mulch into a meadow, and the first California native plants are starting to bloom! Closeups: wartleaf ceanothus, pitcher sage, and blueblossom. (If you're just tuning in, this was a grass lawn 3 years ago)

Sugar Magnolia Tendril Peas! Attractive mottled pale green plants with fuschia flowers make a show in the spring garden before warmer weather crops take off. A kid approved favorite, excellent for snacking and stir-fry. The blossoms are said to symbolize happiness and success. 🌱💚

Let’s take our minds off soggy, swamp-like gardens with summer roses.

man, I have this lovely orchid that has been in bloom for six solid weeks — big three inch lime green and white flowers, and every time I wake up in bed, I can smell it, subtle and soft and the most beautiful white floral I’ve ever smelled

YALLL!!!!! This was the first plant I bought after moving to Utah in 2021. After several attempts, there are two claws sprouting roots!!!! It’s finally propagating!!! Ahhh I’m so excited and so scared!

well here it is bluesky, the first ever things i have grown that are probably edible. keep this in your mind as a milestone for next year when i harvest thousands of potatoes and distribute them exclusively to the poorest people in my neighbourhood

Cucamelons were my sleeper hit this year. They utterly failed on me last year.🌱

All of a sudden, I’ve got a TON of them and they’re awesome! Kids love them, fantastic in salads (they don’t go soggy), amazing pickled. 

Next year, I’ll trellis them on purpose😬. 

Saving seeds…..

Got up this morning at ass o'clock & immediately tore out & replanted half my garden. I'm exhausted & feel creaky, but I'll have a very fun autumn harvest full of Hakurei turnips, red choi, spinach, lacinato kale, cabbage, radicchio, radishes, puntarelle, etc.

I don’t get many data points on The Sorrento Gambit — can I grow Sorrento lemons at home from seed — but I got a good one today. I was carrying them outside from their indoor overwintering home, and their crushed leaves smell *exactly* like the lemon rinds did. 🤩🤩🤩🌱

Living room garden update 🌱 All the lettuce on the shelf has been harvested at least once so that's all regrowth and now I have little tomatoes and peppers starting everywhere This whole thing was a happy accident or a lazy gardener but I'm glad to have had the experience none the less

My poor sweet potato sadly got a fungal infection amongst the foliage it just couldn't shake off, but I will absolutely replant some of the baby tubers and give them a fresh start.

These were originally the end of a grocery store sweet potato! 🌱

I’m super impressed with these Indian Summer black eyed susans. They’ve been blooming since June and are not only still going on Halloween, but are mostly unfazed by the hard freezes we’ve had. Definitely something I’m adding to the 2024 seed lineup! 🌱

Here are some other dahlia seedlings in my garden, ones that I'll probably pull.
The goal with dahlias is a closed face (no yellow pollen disc showing), so you want the ones with loads of petals. These are all second year seedlings, so unlikely to get much more appealing. 🌱🇳🇿

I have a small allotment plot here in Harrow, London as I don't have a garden. My posts will mostly be about the plot and show what I'm growing there, including plenty of flowers such as cosmos, pot marigolds and sunflowers. I also post regularly on my Flighty's plot blog.

I planted bulbs yesterday.

Every year that feels like an active practise of hope.

In the spring, when they bloom, I will think back on yesterday.

My hope is that things will have gotten better. That the day I planted bulbs was also a turning point, even one far too late.

1/2

Hey fellow gardeners 🌱 Anyone try growing luffas before? It might be because it’s been so wet here and not the warmest growing season, but I have no flowers so I assume that means no luffas? Pretty decent vines though. Suggestions?

Note to self...do more research before planting a tree. Case in point: Wanted lovely fast-growing tree to shade house. Disappointed when realized Kentucky coffee tree is leafless almost 9 months of yr & extremely messy once it starts dropping huge leaves (60-90 cm & largest of any Canadian tree). 🌱

🌱To play the long game I also bought some Amaryllis seeds. The seller described them as, "xjohnsonii x La Paz/ cybister". Since I have a real love for cybister Amaryllis, but hate their prices, I thought I'd throw the dice and grow some from seeds.

Primroses also kicking off in Rakesden, along with yesterday's hellebores. Spring is definitely starting to spring, and with buds showing green on this and that, pruning has already started--a bunch of St John's worts getting hard pruned today to clear off all last year's dead leaves and berries. 🌱

The grow tent and second shelf are up. Alas we aren't growing anything more interesting in there than eggplants, peppers, and tomatoes. Taking a break and then getting all the new lights set up. BIG level up from last year's indoor sowing area.

sowing Canna seed today ..all the years  have been gardening and teaching horticulture i had never grown them form seed until last year , but of a faff as you have to Nic the seed , which have a very very hard coat and its easy to cut your finger off then they have to be soaked in water for a week

🌸 downy lobelia 🌿

little late-season lobelia with just one flower left, great opportunity to get a good look at its structure. through the split separating the two small upper lobes you can see the corolla tube that supports the reproductive parts of the flower

Roses as far as the eye can see! Newly-potted roses at Allensmore Nurseries, despatching over the coming weeks to garden centres countrywide. These well-grown plants show why we are so proud of British grown roses!

Picked the majority of my peppers today! It's going down to like 2° tonight with a slight chance of frost so it looks like the growing season is about over here.

This Mandevilla used to climb a trellis over the old pond that is now empty. Had been neglected but now fed, watered and in a semi shade spot it has come back to life.
Still lots of warm weather to come so growth and flowering should keep improving.
Certainly worth saving.

A few years ago I bought some pawpaws (Asimina triloba) because I wanted to try growing them in the inland Northwest. Two of the four grafted trees have survived, but they are STRUGGLING. The rootstock survived on one of the dead ones and it is growing so much better than the selected stock. 😡🌱

Camellias, rhododendrons, azaleas are all starting to bud. Soon these fabulous flashy flowers will run riot in the garden bringing it back to life. Even the magnolias are in green sheathed bud already. 
Spring bulbs also showing their leaves & daffs are already out. I love Spring!

I repotted this little guy this morning. This is the first orchid I've ever gotten from Trader Joe's. It was potted in this dense foamy material and the root system was almost non-existent. I was going to wait until it finished blooming but I couldn't stand to see it in that plastic pot any longer.

This was our first year with some allotment space and while we ended up with so much veg (beets! all of the courgettes! radishes! tomatoes! peppers!), I am mostly proud of my pumpkins, with the one on the left weighing in at 12 lbs.

"The potted pepper plant has been growing remarkably well in it's new pot. The first few peppers finally started to ripen, showing a brilliant red color that fades into a darker scarlet. But most curious of all is the spirals that grow into the pepper"

Planted the last of some chamomile flowers I saved years ago at the old place. Haven't been able to get them started up here yet, but also haven't tried starting them indoors first. Ordering new seeds soon, but wanted to see if these old ones would grow 💚 🌱

i've got a classic red metal radio flyer wagon with a broken axle here and i'm thinking if i knock some holes in the bottom i could use it as a planter but obviously it would have to be something with quite shallow roots and drought tolerant as i imagine it will dry out quick in the sun. ideas? 🌱

I do enjoy regrowing green onions on the window sill. But after three such growths per bulb, I usually re-plant it out in the wild and leave it to itself. I figure it's earned its freedom from food production slavery at that point.

The rain is driving us ‘Koko Loko’! Which is also the name of this uniquely coloured, sweet-scented floribunda rose.
Whatever your weather - have a good day!

Are those green flowers? Yep. These are Edwards Plateau five-eyes. They are summer bloomers but if you couldn’t tell from my other posts, we’re headed in that direction fast & early this year. A Chamaesaracha sp., this flower is native to central and south central Texas, preferring rocky habitats. 🌿

I got rid of well over a hundred strawberry plants from my garden this year (charity fundraiser, I needed space for veges) and I’m still harvesting this many a day right now. 
I went out a little late, and picked a few that could have done with one more day, but there will be loads tomorrow too.

Feeling like hot garbage so I’m amusing myself by looking for homes for the native persimmon trees I started from seed last fall. It’s working! I have two people wanting two seedlings each and I feel so happy about my neighbors’ future food security and the beauty of free stuff. 🌱

One thing that's really taking some getting used to living here is to not buy too much produce because a lot just grows in the garden, so picked the first courgettes before we go to market today to remind myself we have food at home

🌱Whew, so much garden work today; catching up after the Heat Dome in TX means sun-up to sundown yard chores. 🥵

May I be blessed with an abundance of tomatoes, beets, carrots, cabbage and parsnips, ramen.

our three year old dátil pepper is putting out adorable tiny fruit this early in the season - this is a very, very hot (but not ultra-hot) fruity variety from St. Augustine, Florida. Delicious, I love these guys.

We have got into the habit of planting an avocado stone in a decorative vase each year, so you can see the roots. I took a picture of last year's one in the same window. Just something about the way the light plays on them appeals to me.

I'm pretty sure this beloved rose (nicknamed Murder Rose because of his terrifying thorns) died last fall. There's no sign of life but I'm hoping his roots might still be alive. He had orange blooms that aged to yellow and vibrant pink. Here are some past photos. 🌱

🌱Oh, my garlic is already sprouting. 
Planted them at the end of October, but apparently it’s warm enough that they grow well.
But- frost will come, and if it’s anything like this year it’ll hit hard around February. 
So I‘ll definitely need to mulch them more 👀

In Sydney for the next few weeks, and it’s noticeable how much more common brush turkeys are here in the suburbs than 10 or 20 years ago. The jacarandas are a sight (in October, thanks to the very warm spring; traditionally they don’t bloom until November.)

This is an amalgamation of a few pictures of what I now refer to as my “beautiful chaos tent” The cucumber is almost a foot long and I get a few cherry tomatoes every so often lettuce almost as needed and some peppers and other surprises will be ready soon 🌱

It was a gift from someone who knew I wanted a huge jade and brought it back on a trip.

Would you like some flowers? You’d like some flowers. These are all dahlias in the Halifax Public Gardens. If you live in or around Halifax and have not yet gone to marvel in the huge variety of dahlias they have, you need to go!

Sunchokes (Helianthus tuberosus) and sunflowers (H. annuum) can hybridize. The difficulty is that sunchokes bloom so late.

Fortunately, the giant sunflowers had a few secondary flowers that were shedding pollen. Now I just need to watch for seeds.

I'm not sure if this is an effect of the indoor growing conditions or if this is actually a mutation. We'll have to see how the other plant matures (these should be maturing from yellow to a bright red, not brownish-orange.) 🌱

This is the year of giving up on plants for me. I've been giving away a bunch of largish plants that I have dutifully dragged in and out of the house for years and cared for but honestly never really liked. I'm keeping the barrel cactuses but the succulents are going.

🌱 By the time I can get the tomatoes to climb near the top of the hoops, it is usually the end of the season. This year, I wasn't trying hard to get them to climb. I pruned lightly and still had to secure some of the branches with twine.

The volunteer black raspberry brambles 🌱 were threatening to engulf the garage, so I did some pruning yesterday while it was still cool.

The after picture isn't the best. I'll get a better one at a different time of day!

This is another of my favorite plants. It flowers in mid summer on leafless stalks with up to 10 flowers that open successively. They are sometimes called pink naked ladies. This one is a rarer hot pink variety. 🌱

Amaryllis belladonna

I've bagged my first salad of greens from the garden! 🌱
 These are the straggler greens between my marigolds. They're the only ones spared by the marauding bunnies, so it may taste of marigold (which I don't mind). 🏵️

First white dandelion of the spring.  We grow dandelions for chicken treats and occasional salad greens, but it gets boring growing the same old yellow ones.  Taraxacum albidum for the botany geeks. 🌱

This week: Ericaceae are a cosmopolitan family with diverse habits. The morphological innovation that unites them all is the presence of appendages on the anthers, seen here on this dissected flower of Vaccinium pallidum. #Ericaceae #Botany #PlantScience 🌾🧪

Not too much color in the garden currently so on a trip to get milkweed, I couldn't resist some African daisies, pink guara, and a provence lavender. I hope the butterflies enjoy the milkweed among the mistplant this year.

Will look so much better once I get everything mulched! 🌱

🌱 Gardening friends, what was the best tomato you have ever grown, or one that you grow year after year? 

Starting my tomato list for next year. Looking for ones with good taste and disease resistance. Cold tolerance would be nice, too, but not a must have trait. Many thanks!

Got a first time bloomer in my garden here, and she is amazing. This one is At Last and its vibrant orange/apricot is really stunning. She's also blessed with a super strong sweet true rose fragrance that's just out of this world. Another new favourite! 🧡🌼😊💫

🌱 This was the very last tomatoes harvested over the weekend before the first frost. I’ve  also harvested some green ones to ripen in the garage. It had been a very good year in the garden.  And now, goodbye.

The only ripe ones so far are the volunteer orange cherry tomatoes. L-R Aubrey's Special Pink, Unknown heirloom from local organic farm, forgotten orange cherry variety volunteer from last year's planting, and Porter.

Menyanthes trifoliata L., Bogbean, flowers in late May. I know of 2 populations, one on the village pond, planted, & a wild pop. in the local fen/bog/mire (right). A monotypic genus, it has 2 morphs, like Primula, pin-eyed & thrum-eyed. The cultivated ones are pin-eyed, & the wild ones, thrum-eyed.

Rescued this tiny coleus I spotted in the flower patch. I did this last year before frost and enjoyed the goofy plant indoors all winter. It put up an absurd flower stalk full of tiny periwinkle blooms 😄🌱

And here are some proper tuber grown dahlias with names. Except for that purple decorative one, that was a mislabeled one. It has a name, but I don't know it.
- Hollyhill Spiderwoman (cactus)
- Purple Decorative (mystery)
- Fancy Pants (orchid type)
- Fleurel (decorative the size of a dinnerplate)

Just bought 2 “Golden Opportunity” roses for our front trellises on the house to either side of the bay window. It’s got a strong scent and is a strong re-bloomer!
Our house is a yellow leaning cream, with sage green trim and red brick and doors. It’s perfect.

Incidentally HCR has stock!! Gogogo 🌱

I had a couple handfuls of Two-Row Stonecrop on the property in a shady spot. Moved them to this sunny area and BLAMMO. They’ve filled in nicely AND have pretty pink flowers. They’re also amazing at weed suppression (even crab & quack grass).

It doesn't look like much, but I'm quite proud of it 😌🌱

Also I was SO PROUD to get this one bell pepper fully red before it rotted like the others.. but then the threat of frost came and I had to take it in 🥺 We almost got there, little buddy. I'm proud of you. ❤️

Two of our favourite allotment crops, Florence fennel and celeriac. The fennel was sown early to get a good start to harvest bulbs in June before it bolts, celeriac was sown early because it takes until October for the tiny seedlings to finally produce 1-2 kg roots.
#Allotment 🌱

Was growing suspicious that our aji Amarillo chilis are actually something else, but apparently they just take forever to turn yellow. (The plant is very prolific and otherwise seems happy, so, I guess no big deal?)

The green ones still taste great too.

It's December 31st and the Mexican and Californian annuals are still going strong here in Vancouver, while last year's borage is blooming again having not died off with a first frost that never happened 🌱

Took these yesterday, it’s pouring down today. I have my first lemon. Turtle told me to not let it fruit for the first year and I was pulling off all of the blossoms but I must have missed this one. Since it has made it this far I thought I’d let it keep growing. 🌱🇳🇿 Miss you my friend. 🐢♥️

The rains from Hurricane Hillary in August have created a sort of second spring here in the Mojave Desert. Plants that typically bloom in spring and summer, like creosote and sand verbena, are flowering in October. It's beautiful but also a bit unsettling tbh

🌱 And the second flush of tomatoes for the season begins rolling in! (I didn’t succession plant these, they just grew to full size a month ago and refused to ripen - some of the things I actually did succession plant are also turning around the same time, annoyingly)

Okay. It was worth the long, long wait. One blossom today, probably two or three tomorrow, dozens to come over the next week.

Don’t feel the camera does the color justice here; it’s absolutely brilliant.

More of my garden stuff. Tiny eggplants are Thai lavender frog eggplants photo bombed by some long beans that are drying on the vine. Second eggplant is ichiban eggplant. The basket is some lemon thyme and genovese basil I harvested. 🌱

I see many stunning hellebore and cyclamen posts from bsky gardeners, so I set out to find some today. Also got a dainty lavender tree and saw a legion of cacti which I did not purchase but had to photograph. 🌱

Rose season has begun! Every year I add one or two lovely David Austin roses to the collection and it turns out when you do basic stuff like regular fertilising, watering and aphid control you get happy #roses 😄🌱

I found this flowering with 2 more buds to come hopefully. What I like about Paphs & Phrags is that they are less fussy about hard water. 

Paphiopedilum insigne v. sanderianum

#orchids #plants #gardening

This year’s project was to cover up the drab grey walls in our yard.

I built a trellis and planted some runner beans, hoping there was enough sun to get them to grow - this is the shadiest area, with maybe a single hour of direct sun first thing in the morning.

It’s better than I’d ever hoped! 🌱

Here in the UK we’re starting the week with political shenanigans & stormy weather. We’re just going to focus on the roses! 
R. Macrantha. Single fragrant blush pink blooms fading to white. Red hips. Forms an arching shrub with long trailing branches. 
Seen at Mottisfont Rose Garden, Hampshire. 🌱

🌱Six on Saturday this week is tomatoes reaching for the sky in my greenhouse while producing tomatoes, a bee enjoying the violas, a strawberry growing on the patio, dianthus starting to bloom and one of my Hellebore getting ready to explode into bloom 🌱😃#gardenfeed #flowerreport

I did accomplish something today: I moved my peonies and spread them out, then planted a small bit of tulips and daffodils where the peonies used to be

alt text: a garden bed 1) being planted with dormant plants 2) being covered with soil and straw

Spending some time with the Path of the Panther Education Team as we ideate new curriculum ideas for grades 5-HigherEd.  And as we explored the lands of the headwaters of the Florida Everglades we came across this blooming Pine Lily.  These flowers tolerate wet, hot, acidic soils. 🧪🌎

I got these black-eyed Susans on sale at Lowe’s. I’ll put them in the garage at night through the winter & plant them out in Spring. No sooner did I put them out for some sunshine, & these butterflies paid a visit. 🌱

I pruned my roses today (in much too warm sunshine for Feb — hard to complain when it’s so pleasant but climate change is real and terrible). I’m getting excited for spring. My hellebore are blooming, I’ve got more crocus this week, and some daffodils starting to bud out. And of course: weeds. 🌱

This small white aster is, I think, the plant called Small White Aster. It is doing a good job of being a small white aster. Not sure whether it's a wildflower or got here intentionally, but it's pretty and the bees like it. 🌱

I liked Poor Things a lot: it captures the topsy-turviness of Alasdair Gray's brain, his acerbic wit, and his horrors at how cruel human beings can be. Like Frankenstein, both the book & the film ask us who the real monsters are.

Saw Poor Things yesterday and thought it was an extremely interesting movie with lots to chew on about gender and identity in a patriarchal structure. The sets were amazing too, particularly all the weird hodgepodge creatures.

In 2004, mathematicians at King's College London conducted a study commissioned by Sky Movies which concluded that THE SHINING was the 'perfect scary movie' due to a proper balance of elements including shock value, suspense, escalating music and isolated setting.

Just finished Poor Things (2023). I’ve seen most of Lanthimos’s previous films, and they were good, but I’ve had a bit of trouble connecting with them. I had no such issue here. This is marvelous stuff. Visually incredible, this is one of the best looking movies I’ve seen in a quite a while.

PASSAGES (streaming on Mubi) is a really well-done, excruciating slow burn film about the messiness of relationships — especially when they begin falling apart. 

It’s 90 mins of beautiful people having sex and/or behaving terribly. Just know that you’ll never look at Paddington the same way again

One of my favourite "vibes" horror films. There is a plot and characters but none of that matters. What matters is the terrifying goblin score and gorgeous lighting, dreamlike editing. It's a rather beautiful nightmare made real.

This was pointed out in an AV Club write-up for the greatest horror movies of the 80s, but it bears repeating, as it sure feels right: The Thing is the greatest special effects movie ever made.

The way I see it, there isn't a bit of CGI work out there that can't compare w/ the practical FX here.

📽️

I've never heard anyone say MAD MAX (1979) is the best MAD MAX film, and it's not, but it warrants re-evaluation. It's scarily relevant.

MAD MAX isn't post-apocalyptic. It's about how the apocalypse sets in, as society gradually dies around us. It's about giving up on the fantasy of civilization. 📽️

I just saw TALK TO ME at the theater and holy fuck this is supernatural horror filmmaking of the highest order. Cannot recommend it highly enough if you’re looking for a scare accompanied by a palpable sense of dread.

Also watched Talk To Me, which was excellent. Really creative and dread-inducing small scale horror. Performances were outstanding and the ghost scenes are grotesquely creative. It’s an A24 movie, so you know you’re getting something unusual and high quality.

Love Lies Bleeding was everything I hoped it would be. One of the absolute best modern sapphic films, equally hot, passionate, toxic and uncomfortable. It's complex and messy and never shies away from taking the most visceral punch possible. Absolutely loved it, best film I've seen this year.

Shocked THE BOYS NEXT DOOR isn't better known. Spheeris' film is certainly worthy of more attention/acclaim.  Among other things, it's a great example of the "female gaze." Seldom do you see the camera so entranced with the beauty of a movie's male stars.  It's also a film of rare tenderness/empathy

MOONLIGHT (2016)
Dir. Barry Jenkins
United States
Drama, Queer

A look at three defining chapters in the life of Chiron, a gay Black man growing up in Miami. His journey is guided by the kindness of the community that helps raise him as he experiences the pain and beauty of falling in love.

Yeah, Poor Things is a 5 out of 5 for me. Not much of the clipped bleakness I normally go to a Lanthimos film for but a five star film is a five star film, wouldn't change nothing. The discourse will be  insufferable

So, now I know why M3GAN is a gay icon. Spooktober Moviefest night 11 was M3GAN! Finally got around to seeing it and I was not disappointed! It was fun and a little campy at times. Definitely a fun watch!

STARRY EYES (2014)
Dir. Kevin Kolsch, Dennis Widmyer
United States
Horror, Drama, Fantasy

A hopeful young starlet uncovers the ominous origins of the Hollywood elite and enters into a deadly agreement in exchange for fame and fortune.

#NowWatching Poor Things (2023) - A wonderful Frankenstein picaresque. Lanthimos continues to be one of the most interesting filmmakers working. I’m a little too tired and scattered this morning to really dig in, but following are a few (spoiler free) things that made me extremely happy: #filmsky /1

Haunted Mansion is absolutely everything I could have wanted given that it wasn’t straight horror. It’s a great gateway horror movie that pays a lot of respect to the lore of the attraction and its creators while addressing grief and loss. I loved it so much!

Now watching

Notorious shocker from 1970 in which a young model comes to stay with a creepy photographer and his randy bisexual aunt.  Bad things ensue.  This was the debut film of director Jose Ramon Larraz, BOTD in 1929.

Angel Heart [1987] dir: Alan Parker
A slow burn with an obvious spoiler right at the start but still an enjoyable 'thriller'. Parker excels here with the Trevor Jones score and Courtney Pine's saxophone adding texture to the lush cinematography. Still consider this Mickey Rourke's best performance.

PARASITE (2019)
Dir. Bong Joon-Ho
South Korea
Comedy, Thriller, Drama

All unemployed, Ki-taek's family takes peculiar interest in the wealthy and glamorous Parks for their livelihood until they get entangled in an unexpected incident.

2001, a space odyssey (1968)

hadn’t seen it before until recently! still shocked that a movie as aged as this one could be so breathtaking, suspenseful, and inspiring, while also being one of the first of its kind.

Looking for something unique? Check out TETSUO: THE IRON MAN

Likened to the work of David Lynch and David Cronenberg, TETSUO molds explosive violence, bizarre sexual imagery and jet-black humor into a cinematic experience unlike any you've seen.

My favorite movie from this year about a naïve woman who goes on an adventure around the Real World with a swarthy himbo companion learning harsh lessons about Patriarchy and Feminism before returning home and creating the life she wants for herself? It's called POOR THINGS

All of us Strangers is probably the most mature and nuanced gay film about adult romance and trauma to come out of North America in…forever?  But it didn’t get an Oscar nom because it’s actually for gay people.

4 horror films to know me:

Texas Chainsaw Massacre (stone cold classic, flawless)
The Return of the Living Dead
Slumber Party Massacre
Killer Klowns from Outer Space

because if there’s one thing I’m going to love, it’s a horror comedy!!

such a beautiful & elegant minute-plus long crane shot in the funhouse. tracks the little brother making his way towards the exit as stragglers clear out. captures unspoken confusion of this kid by considering space around him. this kid's experience on this night is the best stuff in the film imo

Delighting in one of my true comfort films, Pacific Rim, put through a monochrome filter

Stripped of colour you're left with only contrasts, with details, and you see the film as something different. Harsher and raw in a beautiful way
More Metropolis than monster thriller

Thoroughly enjoyed You Hurt My Feelings. I’m getting old.
Smartly written comedies about people doing the best they can and aren’t trying to be edgy or push boundaries are my go to movies anymore and this is a good one. See also this year’s Asteroid City.

Y TU MAMÁ TAMBIÉN (2001)
Dir. Alfonso Cuarón
Mexico
Drama, Romance, Comedy

A pair of horny Mexico City teenagers and a gorgeous older Spanish woman embark on a road trip and form an increasingly intense and sensual alliance.

Talk to Me is a solid-A horror movie. Loved it. It coulda been an A+ if they had titled it Talk To The Hand… I had to shave three points off for such an obvious unforced error. Otherwise, just perfect.

started this movie the other day bc of the chair memes and i could not stop watching my fucking god dude 

i expected it to maybe be good

but i didn't expect to be an entire sloppy sobbing mess. what a fucking great, heartwarming watch 10/10

Finally saw WHERE THE DEVIL ROAMS, from The Adams Family. Dark, brooding period piece about a murderous family of carnival workers with haunting visuals. Really enjoyed it. Big fan of The Adams Family's work. Coolest family working in indie genre film today. Streaming on Tubi. 🎥 #filmsky #horrorsky

Watched All the Colors of the Dark (1972) tonight… I’m not a big giallo guy, but they’re occasionally very nice as palette cleansers. Anyhow, this is one the good ones. A very paranoid film, very creepy, great soundtrack.

Messiah of Evil (1974) is one of the most beautiful films I’ve seen in a long time. I want to live in it, even if I get violently murdered. It would be a good looking death. No idea what’s happening most of the time, don’t care

enraptured by the lurid pink/green two-tone technicolor palette of Doctor X (1932).

directed by Michael Curtiz (Casablanca), this pre-code mad scientist horror movie was so clearly one of the many inspirations behind the Rocky Horror Picture Show.

PARASITE (2019)
Dir. Bong Joon-Ho
South Korea
Comedy, Thriller, Drama

All unemployed, the Kim family takes a peculiar interest in the wealthy and glamorous Park clan for their lavish livelihood — until both families get entangled in an unexpected incident.

THE LOST BOYS is my favorite vampire movie ever. It’s got an incredible soundtrack, the two Coreys, and some guy named Kiefer. I love Technicolor films. Say what you want about Shumacher and his wild aesthetics. He sure nailed this one. Sometimes it’s better to stay off the boardwalk.🩸

THE IRON CLAW is fucking amazing. Riveting, heartbreaking, tragic, and glorious. Hands down one of the best movies I’ve seen all year. It’s like Linklater’s THE WRESTLER. Whether you know the story by heart or are hearing it for the first time, you’ll likely be as blown away as I was.

THANKSGIVING: Tawdry, icky and a truly indecent amount of fun. So nice, amidst the current vogues of Elevated Horror and PG-13 entry-level stuff (both producing excellent work!), to see a good old-fashioned slasher. There’s a real sicko glee at work here and thank goodness.

We watched this on Saturday. I knew nothing about it going in and the posters/marketing material don't do it justice, it's a spectacular movie. Shot on location in Italy so looks gorgeous, Matt Damon is sensational, it has a gnarly twist halfway through that rockets it through to the end

The World's Great Sinner (1962)

Absolutely batty film about an insurance salesman who gets canned from his job and becomes a cult leader

Starring, written, and directed by Timothy Carey, BOTD in 1929

Watched Hellboy (2004) tonight and surprised at how well it holds up, in it's clear storytelling & effects. It's nearly 20 years old, but Del Toro's choices with the effects were really good ones, hardly any uncanny shocks. Can't say that for some big budget movies half that age. Recommend.

SEXY BEAST (2000)
Dir. Jonathan Glazer
United Kingdom, Spain
Crime, Comedy, Drama, Thriller

Retired safecracker Gal Dove is living the good life on the southern Spanish Coast, until his past reappears in the form of a psychotic gangster intent on bringing Gal out of retirement for one last job.

Not only is this one of the best horror movies I’ve ever seen, it’s one of the best movies I’ve ever seen. You have to watch The Shining and Doctor Sleep back to back for that PUNCH. It MASSIVELY improved the original, enriching the lore & not being vague abt Jacks demise. 10/10 #horrorsky #filmsky

PASSAGES (2023)
Dir. Ira Sachs
France
Drama, Queer, Romance

Tomas and Martin are a gay couple living in Paris whose marriage is thrown into crisis when Tomas impulsively begins a passionate affair with young schoolteacher Agathe.

“Jane Greer, Ida Lupino, Robert Ryan dine together at San Francisco's Embarcadero restaurant before the world premiere of ‘Hard, Fast And Beautiful.’” — Screenland, Oct. 1951

Lupino produced and directed this movie. Ryan had a cameo with Lupino in it.

#Watched Lupin III: The Mystery of Mamo (1978) - not the best or weirdest Lupin flick I’ve seen, but by far the HORNIEST. When Lupin is hooked up to a brainwave scanner, his brain is like 90% boobs.

Dug the funky style though, way more pop art than Cagliostro’s lush mittel-Europa. #filmsky

I watched Michael Caine thriller The Black Windmill (Don Siegel, 1974).

It'd just be an ok, slow-burn spy/actioner, but stylish direction, a great cast filled out w top character actors (see alt text), & excellent location work, incl a hovercraft crossing of the Channel, make it quite a lot of fun.

homage to 'invitation to hell' wes craven tv movie, shot it right after he did nightmare. about a hole to hell hidden inside a country club. robert urich builds a heat proof suit to travel down inside, his family are replaced with demons

Spooktober Moviefest night 4: Knock at the Cabin. This one was a bit emotionally visceral for me. The relationship between the main actors is beautiful and I saw a lot of myself and my husband in that. It was a tough watch but really good overall.

Well…I knew I was expecting something at the end of Saltburn, but I was not quite expecting that. The whole film: Exquisite art direction, exquisite score, exquisite performance by Barry Keoghan. And exquisite Irish willy 🥵😍

For international women's day, here are four exploitation films directed by women. 📽️

Terminal Island - Stephanie Rothman

Stripped to Kill - Katt Shea

The Slumber Party Massacre - Amy Holden Jones

Kissed - Lynne Stopkewich

So I saw #TalkToMe last night and it’s one of the scariest films I’ve seen in years. 100% worth seeing in the theater. The trailer doesn’t do it justice and if you trust me just go see it without watching the trailer so it’s all a surprise.

WHEN EVIL LURKS is the best horror movie I've seen this year. Like a twisted and brutal occult detective story, it lays out the rules for the characters and punishes those who deviate from protocol. Heavy-duty trigger warnings abound, so please check the label before proceeding.

Talk to Me is hands down the best horror I’ve seen in a long time. The concept/story is intriguing. good paced storytelling. Characters are well built. Chemistry is on spot. Scare scenes make sense, not just random pointless jump scares. And I got a good chill in the end. Plus the Aussie elements 🤣👌

To BAFTA for RAGING GRACE, which is going to be one of the films of the year. Mother-daughter immigrant story that becomes a thriller that becomes racially-charged horror. And that great rarity, a British genre film. General release soon, seek it out.

This weekend's watch. SEX, LIES & VIDEOTAPE (1989).  Haven't seen it in 25 years at least. Forgot how good it is.

Made for a scant $1M, it won the grand prize at Cannes, & Spader won best actor. Brilliantly written. Marvelous characters.

If you haven't watched it in awhile, highest recommendation.

📽️🍿 I thought SATAN'S SLAVES was a very effective & creepy movie, but hot damn did they upgrade the horror with COMMUNION. Intense & atmospheric, this sequel was an eerie joy to watch. Disturbing, unnerving, and filled with supremely scary shit- I highly recommend them BOTH.

📽️ filmsky horror movie

the amazing mr x is a twisty, highly-atmospheric horror/thriller from eagle-lion films about a phony spiritualist. it’s pretty great… pretty low budget but the photography is beautiful and the script is tight and clever. shades of the uninvited and nightmare alley….this one is legit. check it out. 📽️

MANDY is an incredible cinematic masterpiece. The camerawork is alluring. The colors are so warm! I love that it’s done in acts. It’s a brilliant artistic film that also happens to be an awesome horror flick. The characters are engaging. The plot has some very tender moments. 📽️🕸️🍿🗃️

You *will* be moved by a horror comedy featuring a very-much-alive-yet-elderly Elvis defending his nursing home from a soul sucking mummy with the help of JFK (who is also still alive -- and black!). Can The King regain his long buried mojo and save the residents of Shady Rest? Don't bet against it.

Bringing Out The Dead doesn't get its due, both as a Scorsese film and as a film capturing the spirit of the late 90s, with possibly Nicholas Cage's best performance. 

It's surprisingly gothic in atmosphere and manages to capture Scorsese's twin passions of New York grime and human spirituality.

One of the best horror films I've ever seen. A genuinely creepy premise superbly realised. Go in knowing as little as possible and prepare to have your mind blown. It has one of the most powerful opening scenes ever. And it's as Aussie as roadkill kangaroo without any cultural cringe.Just brilliant.

Astaire and Rogers in Top Hat. One of the most platonically perfect movies I’ve ever seen. The dancing is at the  ‘impossible’ level of difficulty. One is reminded of watching Bruce Lee. That these two should also be great at screwball comedy boggles the mind.

Thief (1981) is so much better than Heat (1995), not only for omitting the disgusting and unnecessary Waingro sequences—scenes whose only function is "proving the bad guys are bad" and inevitably the proof is violence against working women, but I think it has a different view of commitment

Shock Treatment (1981) might not be as iconic as it's predecessor Rocky Horror but this almost forgotten sequel sure beats it in set and costume design.
Worth a watch just for the homophobia song that rhymes faggots with maggots.

For Friday the 13th yesterday, I went to see Saw X and it was a great movie! It takes place between the events of the first & second one, we learn more about John Kramer (Tobin Bell is incredible). I enjoyed how they balanced the plot & the gruesomeness of the movie. I’ll make a top 10 soon! 🍿

The Silent Partner (1978)

An edge of your seat Christmastime thriller.  One of the best movies set in Toronto.

Directed by Daryl Duke

Starring Elliot Gould and Christopher Plummer, who was BOTD in 1929

Serie Noire (1979)

A superb, bleakly hilarious film noir about a door-to-door salesman who is lured into crime by a teenage prostitute.  Based on the Jim Thompson novel A Hell of a Woman.  

Directed by Alain Corneau

Screenplay by Corneau and Georges Perec, who was BOTD in 1936

The City of the Dead AKA Horror Hotel (1960)

One of the great Gothic horror movies

Directed by John Llewellyn Moxey

Starring Christopher Lee, Vanetia Stevenson, and Betta St. John, who was BOTD in 1929

ENTRAILS OF A BEAUTIFUL WOMAN is fucking insane. Ultra pinky violence and not for everyone. Really gnarly effects in but never saw someone die in a horror film by a woman sitting on a man’s face and suffocating him? Holy shit. Last 20 min is a fucking trip. Loved it.

"Talk To Me" on Netflix is a cracking little horror movie. I went into it thinking it was going to be the usual thing - bunch of pretty American teenagers running and screaming. It's Australian and SO much better than that. Actually pretty scary. Loved the ending.

Miller’s Girl (2024) Instant Classic. Simmering. 𝘯𝘰𝘵 𝘰𝘯𝘭𝘺 tension; but a classical plot line many are failing to grasp. Layering woven thru spoken word, maturation of longing & framing like Minnelli. Literature was vehicle for stealth Gothic Romance. If u don’t like books, you’ll hate this #filmsky

Just got back from seeing End of Evangelion in theaters. It's one of my fav movies, and I've seen it lots of times on video, but never on the big screen until now.

It's such a unique film that will never be repeated. It's grim, cool, visceral, stylish and unapologetically disarming. A masterpiece.

ELLE (2016)
Dir. Paul Verhoeven
France, Germany
Drama, Thriller, Crime, Erotica

When Michèle, the CEO of a gaming software company, is attacked in her home by an unknown assailant, she refuses to let it alter her precisely ordered life, engaging in a game of cat and mouse with her stalker.

Lisa Frankenstein: Kathryn Newton falls for Cole Sprouse's reanimated corpse and starts murdering people to fix him up, in Zelda Williams' macabre rom-com, that shoots for a Tim Burton meets Heathers vibe, but Diablo Cody's script feels weak, and the PG-13 rating blunts the much-needed edge.

This stylish first film by the Cohen Brothers is clearly done as inexpensively as possible, but the plot is as rich and as fully formed as one might expect from them. There’s some fantastic writing, acting, and plot twists throughout. 

…/ 1 of 2

Just saw Talk to Her (2002)… I’ve been gradually working my way through Almodóvar’s filmography, and this is surely the best one. Very strange, very uncomfortable. I admire his ability to grapple with the most problematic concepts.

Porno Erotico Western AKA Porno-Erotic Western (1979) dir. Angelo Pannacciò. Every copy of this in circulation has an SMPTE Timecode and seems to be from the same telecine master, which adds to it's charm:

18/31

INVASION OF THE BODY SNATCHERS (1978) is not only the best film adaptation of the story, it's also one of the most frightening sci-fi movies ever made. A quiet apocalypse, where the end is written on expressionless faces, background shadows, and whispered conspiracies. A horror masterpiece.

Just rewatched Week-End in Havana (1941), which is getting to be one of my comfort movies. It’s one of my favorite Fox musicals… the songs aren’t too good (except for the 3 Carmen Miranda numbers), but it’s a wonderful screwball comedy. All the characters are great and the Technicolor is magnificent

Omg I just saw this for the first time and it's absolutely incredible I'm so obsessed. Low budget and not a lot of plot but it's so action/comedy packed you will not mind.

Made by a married stunt-person couple and what their industry friends, Secret Agent 00 Soul is really a wild ride!

It's funny after all "it's what you DON'T SEE that's scarier!" types pearl clutching over Carpenter's THE THING to rewatch that movie and realize what a generally low key and restrained move it is...until it needs to let things off the chain.

#NunsploitationSunday

No tits in this one, but it does have Sophia Loren sporting a naughty librarian/sexy nun mash-up look and music/actor Adriano Celentano wearing cool as fuck hats.

Bianco, rosso e... AKA The Sin AKA White Sister (1972) dir. Alberto Lattuada:

Last night, I rewatched Lake Mungo for the first time since I saw it maybe 15 years ago. And let me tell you, this movie is effective! I had full-body goosebumps multiple times. No jump scares, just eerie as fuck. Great storytelling, too. I wish more people made them like this

I woke up sobbing this morning. I'd been dreaming about "All Of Us Strangers." I saw this movie a while back, and I can't shake it. It peeled back some things I wasn't prepared to encounter again.
Beautiful and devastating. 
If you haven't seen it, you should.

Dreamchild (1985)

A sadly neglected British fantasy film in which now elderly Alice Hargreaves, the inspiration for Lewis Carroll's Alice books, reflects on her childhood relationship with Carroll.

Screenplay by Dennis Potter

Directed by Gavin Millar, who was BOTD in 1938

GOODFELLAS (1990)
Dir. Martin Scorsese
United States
Crime, Drama

The true story of Henry Hill, a half-Irish, half-Sicilian Brooklyn kid who is adopted by neighborhood gangsters at an early age and climbs the ranks of a Mafia family under the guidance of Jimmy Conway.

Watched The Doorway to Hell (1930) this afternoon… an early Warner Bros gangster film, and James Cagney’s 2nd film appearance. Not especially notable, except for how great Mr Cagney is. He’s every inch the gangster tough guy here, it’s obvious why they quickly started giving him lead roles.

I watched Terminator 2 last night for the first time since 1991. Action scenes still amazing, Robert Patrick still terrifying, Linda Hamilton still badass, all the stuff with the kid still incredibly stupid & poorly acted, final thumb up still the most cringe-inducing thing I’ve ever seen. A++/D-

I never get tired of watching EVERYTHING EVERYWHERE ALL AT ONCE. It's heartwarming, funny, thought-provoking, emotional, mind-blowing, and weird. It's genre-bending, the cinematography is brilliant, and the performances are phenomenal. It's a magnificent movie and Stephanie Hsu deserved that Oscar.📽️

NAKED LUNCH (1991)
Dir. David Cronenberg
Canada

Tonight I saw St Drogo. It’s low-budget, queer, slow-burn, folk horror. I loved the cinematography, and the interesting critical takes on status & community. I also think there’s a lot going on about the nature of desire, and the diverse ways it’s regulated by religion, money, and social groups.

NO ONE WILL SAVE YOU pits dangers from above against those rooted deep in the soul, following Kaitlyn Dever as she wordlessly fights to survive extraterrestrial horrors while struggling to admit that she has any right to do so.

Terrifying.

Heartbreaking.

This is how it’s done.

We just watched The Full Monty. Damn, does that movie ever hold up! Men (of the industrial persuasion) being supportive of each other. Discussion about body positivity.
A queer relationship that is accepted as just ... Business as usual.
Truly incredible, 1997.
It's not super kind to Sheffield tho.

TOTALLY KILLER (2023): Maybe my favorite time travel movie in a minute? Understands that movie quantum physics is a vibe, not a science, and introduces a few fun new wrinkles into the mix. Owes a lot to FREQUENCY, oddly enough?

Just watched Eileen (2023)… a good little showcase for Thomasin McKenzie, a superb young actress I remain super impressed with. A lesbian period piece, it plays around with noir tropes in some interesting ways.

DEATH MACHINE (1994); endearingly loving tribute to the 80s genre flick, loosing a robotic ALIEN into a BLADE RUNNER-esque megacorp HQ during a DIE HARD-style heist. Brad Dourif delightful as an evil tech nerd incel. Stretches its low budget beautifully across its smoky, steely cyberpunk surface.

Just watched The Sky’s the Limit (1943), loved it. Kind of a trifle, plot wise, but Fred gets to do some dramatic acting, and he does it well. Joan Leslie is fine but this is almost like a solo Astaire movie. The scene where he’s drunk and dancing through a bar destroying things is just marvelous.

The Harbinger (2022) is a gem. An effective horror story that examines the pandemic from multiple angles, the film makes great use of its limited budget by leaving some existentially terrifying stuff to the imagination. Stellar, naturalistic performances too

Saw a masterwork from Iran. "The Wasteland", directed by Ahmad Bahrami. 

Deeply indebted to Béla Tarr, shot in gorgeous B&W, with patient long takes. A contemplation on how capitalism has hollowed out the Earth entire. 

Absolutely my favorite film of 2020, if not the decade so far.

A surprisingly human story grounds one of the most visually stunning movies I’ve seen in a while. ILM continues to prove why they are the best. Excellent performances and a great score on top on the amazing visuals. See it on the largest screen possible.

A noir with a weird dream-like vibe and wildly unpredictable structure. Robert Cummings as a down-at-heel ex-military man hired to be chauffeur for an impressively menacing gangster played by Steve Cochran (backed up by Peter Lorre), and falling for the gangster's wife Michèle Morgan.

And that final standoff.

Sergio Leone, Ennio Morricone, and the editing/cinematography combo of Nino Baragli, Eugenio Alabiso, and Tonino Delli Colli at the height of their powers.

Impossibly badass with an energy and a tension that stands unrivaled.

THAT is how you stage a climax for an epic.

Now on Shudder.

Reading Sagan's Pale Blue Dot, and he talks about the Cassini mission, which was still three years away ("if all goes well"), and how it will change what we know—and now here we are, knowing what Cassini saw, and it's marvelous.

The most energetic single particle ever detected, a cosmic ray dubbed the "Oh-My-God" particle, was observed by the Fly's Eye Cosmic Ray Detector OTD in 1991.

Its energy was about 3.2 x 10²⁰ eV ~ 51 J, equivalent to a baseball moving at almost 60 mph. 🧪 🔭

The Mercury-Atlas 6 mission launched OTD in 1962. John Glenn became the first American to orbit Earth, circling three times in the Friendship 7 capsule. 

Glenn refused to trust the computer controlled trajectory until mathematician Katherine Johnson had checked it by hand. 👩🏾‍🔬 🔭

The Pioneer 10 and Pioneer 11, launched in 1972 and 1973 respectively, included gold-anodized aluminum plaques with a message drafted by Carl Sagan in the language of astrophysics--directions for how to find us, and what we look like, should these strange shouts in the dark ever reach another world.

This is another good video for size comparisons. It also shows how dense a black hole is, the gravitational pull of its mass is mind blowing. The way the black hole warps spacetime to generate a nebula that big is honestly incredible.

Fun fact: there are only 9,096 stars in the night sky visible without a telescope. Less than 10,000 even in the most perfectly remote location.

Split between north and south hemispheres, the most perfectly clear night sky you've ever seen had about 4,500 stars visible.

composite image of saturn in the infrared from cassini. the icy rings are blue, thermal radiation from below appears red, backlighting cloud layers, and the aurora of the southern hemisphere is bright green, where particles drawn by the magnetic field precipitate through the atmosphere. 🔭

The James Webb Space Telescope may have found that an exoplanet 124 lightyears away, K2-18b, has water and dimethyl sulphide in its atmosphere. The astronomers won't want to overstate but I'm here to tell you: that means that that planet smells like cabbage.

Some numbers to make you feel very small and precious 🥰❤️
The Milky Way Galaxy contains up to 400 billion stars. 
The Andromeda Galaxy has possibly 1 trillion plus stars. 
The IC 1101 Galaxy contains well over 100 trillion stars. 
But there is YOU❤️ There is me! 
We are small and yet we’re great ❤️💙😘

In time for your bedtime space tweet, NASA drops a new image from the Webb Telescope. "NGC 604 rolling out the red carpet for more than 200 new stars"
NGC 604 is approximately 2.7M light-years away from Earth within the Triangulum Galaxy. Ignore Hollywood, here’s where stars are born.

For Apollo 15, 16, and 17, NASA sent up the battery powered Lunar Roving Vehicle so the astronauts could extend their range as they explored the moon. Weighing only 76 lbs on the moon, the moon buggy helped the astronauts transport equipment and samples around, and all three are still on the moon!

Messier 82- Some galaxies are especially good at generating bright new stars and have so much galactic dust and gas that they create stars  at a furious rate. M82 is also called the “Cigar galaxy” due the red glow of Hydrogen “smoke” emanating from its galactic core.Imaged from my Ottawa backyard.

This time last year saw one of SOFIA's last flights take place at the Edwards Airshow. SOFIA, or the Stratospheric Observatory For Infrared Astronomy first started scientific flights in 2014, averaging roughly one hundred such flights per year.

For years, black holes were just an odd but valid solution to Einstein’s general relativity equations. The *math* predicted them, but nobody knew if they were real. Now, of course, we know that they’re everywhere, including the huge one at the center of our galaxy. Physics is so cool.

Today is the 100th anniversary of the observations that demonstrated the Universe extended far beyond our galaxy. It always blows my mind that our understanding of the Universe on the biggest scales has consistently lagged *way* behind our understanding of the smallest (subatomic) scales. 🧪🔭

And, last pulsar in my session for the evening (and 2023 observing!!!) ... the brightest and closest millisecond pulsar to Earth, PSR J0437-4715. Discovered at Parkes!

This thing is soooo bright across our full wideband receiver, it really is awesome! Also located away from the Galactic plane. 🔭🧪📡

Thinking again about how scientists have evidence for a probable concentration of mass millions of times bigger than our galaxy pulling everything toward it and decided to name it “The Great Attractor” instead of something cool like “The Devouring Sky.”

🔭 Using a Star Analyser 100 with my eVscope to look at Messier 57, the Ring Nebula, to get a spectrum of the entire field of view (including stars). The Ring (left, middle) is a planetary nebula. The Lagoon Nebula in a very purple false color SHO combination. You can see the center of the nebula with your unaided eyes as a small fuzzy patch under very dark skies.

The Triangulum Galaxy (Messier 33) with foreground stars removed. The little specs of lights around it aren't stars, those are other, more distant galaxies photobombing the image. #astrophotography 🧪🔭

🔭 Here's the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mt Wilson Observatory in California. 100" was world's largest from 1917 until the 200" was finished at Palomar. in 1948. It was here that the galaxies were proven to be apart from the Milky Way & expansion of the universe was discovered. #TelescopeTuesday

Vesta, Ceres, and the Moon, to scale.

I love this type of astro paper. @@19288 & T Bakx show simply and convincingly, that high photometric redshifts are biased upwards. Using mostly counting statistics arguments a la Eddington (1913), it's shown that this gets worse with photo-z error.
Bonus points for the title!

The gravitational waves measured by IPTA have wavelengths ~10 light-years.  TEN LIGHT-YEARS! It is literally a galactic scale observatory! Locally (on Earth scales), these GWs don't look like waves, so LIGO can't detect them. You need a galactic observatory for these. WOW.

Happy Rotating Black Hole Day!

Roy Kerr submitted his paper describing the spacetime around a rotating black hole OTD in 1963.

Physicists had been searching for decades for this solution to Einstein's field equations. It describes, at least approximately, every black hole in the Universe. 🧪 🔭

Mars is a stunning fossil. The planet isn’t geographically active like Earth. 

A new study reports the discovery of cracks in the Martian mud that are billions of years old. This means ancient Mars had wet-dry cycles, which suggests the possibility of life. 1/2

In 1977 the Voyager 1 probe was launched on a path which led out of the solar system. It carried a record with images of Earth's life forms.

Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar was born OTD in 1910. He established an upper limit on the mass of stable white dwarf stars. Above this Chandrasekhar Limit – about 1.44 solar masses – stars eventually explode and then collapse into a neutron star or black hole. 🧪 🔭

Voyager 2 was launched into deep space 46 years ago this month. It was expected to last 5 years, but is still going. Voyager 2 lost contact last month when the wrong command turned its antenna away from Earth. Today, its "heartbeat" signal was detected, so this amazing craft is still at work.

Acrylic on canvas I posted for #SpacetoberChallenge Day 8: Star Cluster. The Pleiades is an open cluster ~444 light years from Earth. The cluster contains ~3,000 stars, but only a handful can be seen by eye. The cluster is important for many cultures around the world. (1/2)

An image taken with a 8.2" telescope. It's a 17m exposure, 17 arcmin on a side, limiting mag V ~20, 4 arcsec pixels. It doesn't look like much. But the telescope is on New Horizons out in the Kuiper belt. It has the darkest sky ever seen. Great for the cosmic optical background of the Universe.

Supernovae forge a Moon's worth of gold, but kilonovae, born from neutron star collisions, churn out a Jupiter's worth! Despite our galaxy seeing ~100-1000x more SN than KN, KN contribute >10x more gold!
Your gold ring? A cosmic treasure made of stuff forged in the hearts of colliding neutron stars.
