🔭 Last night a 7th magnitude star photobombed Jupiter and the four Galilean moons. Actually, the Jupiter system moved past the star as seen from our vantage point on Earth. The star is 650 light years away, while Jupiter is 33 light minutes from Earth.

Then I read the term "mean-motion orbital resonance" which is when 2 bodies have revolutions that are a "simple integer ratio of each other" (per wiki.) When 2 bodies have mean-motion orbital resonance, they either stabilize (sync up but never touch) or destabilize (one gets ejected).

Firefly Aerospace has confirmed the launch at 0228 UTC Sep 15 of VICTUS NOX, and Space Force has cataloged object 57861 in a 476 x 531 km x 97.3 deg, 1804 LTDN sun-synch orbit, confirming successful launch. Object's ground track consistent with launch from Vandenberg at the specified time

Eta Carinae lies in the center of the Homunculus Nebula. A star on the verge of supernova, it initially went through a "Great Eruption" in the mid 1800s that ejected stellar gases and formed the nebula seen here. It is the only star known to emit natural LASER light, here in the ultraviolet spectrum

Omega Centauri, globular cluster which resides some 17000 light-years distant. Always good tests for optics: any focus issues and the stars just do not become pinpoint sharp. This is a 4 hour image, one hour each in L, R, G, and B, for a luminance layered shot. Telescope 12.5" RC.

One way to answer the question: Where is the center of the Universe?

You are the center.

Because you're not measuring the entire Universe, but the Observable Universe, which necessarily is centered on the observer.

And you cannot ever know if there's anything beyond 46.5 Bly.

The Ingenuity Helicopter is an autonomous craft that flew to Mars along with the Perserverance rover. While the rover searches for signs of former life, Ingenuity is a test of powered flight on Mars, which has less gravity but an very thin atmosphere!

Psyche separated from the Falcon 9 at 1522 UTC. Both objects are now on an Earth escape trajectory of 794 x -36991 km x 30.1 deg. Coasting on this hyperbola, they will leave the Earth's grav'l sphere of influence on Oct 16 and enter a 1.0 x 2.5 AU x  1.1 deg solar orbit en route to 2026 Mars flyby

New in neat planetary seismology: fifty-year-old seismic records contained the signals of the Apollo 17 lunar module warming and cooling at sunrise and sunset. Congrats to the trio of authors! Imagine what we'll be able to hear with new instruments on the Moon.

This is Messier 82, the "Cigar Galaxy."

It lies 12 million light-years away in the Big Dipper. Thanks to the powerful interaction with the neighbouring galaxy Messier 81 many new stars are born there.

Messier 82 was discovered by Johann Elert Bode on December 31, 1774.

Karl Jansky invented the field of radio astronomy OTD in 1932. 

During a solar eclipse he saw no change in the faint radio hiss he'd been monitoring, ruling out the sun as a source. He soon attributed this "star noise" to large ionized gas clouds near the center of the Milky Way. 🧪 🔭

Santuario Madonna della Corona is a sanctuary in the Italian Alps near Lake Garda. It sits on a sheer cliffside, offering stunning views and housing a revered Black Madonna statue. Pilgrims visit for its tranquil atmosphere and scenic pilgrimage path, drawn by its rich history and natural beauty.

Parish Church of St. Mary, the oldest building in Wittenberg. Luther himself preached here and was married to Katharina von Bora in this triole-aisle Gothic (14C-15C) church that got a neo-Gothic makeover in the 18C. 

#photography 📷 William Schmidt

A megalithic monument in Spain that's older than the Pyramids was recently uncovered from its watery hiding place by a drought. 

The "Spanish Stonehenge" is about 7,000 years old, some 2,000 years older than Stonehenge itself.

The Cloth of St Gereon is the oldest known European tapestry still existing, dating to the early 11th century. Parts of it exist in a number of museums.  The tapestry formerly hung in the choir of St Gereon's Church, Cologne.

Architecture POTD: The Medici Prittie Palace, Florence Italy.

This is thee Art History textbook example of Renaissance architecture, and as such it's one of the most iconic buildings in the world. I can't even believe that I got to see it in person; it's just that big of a deal in architecture.

County Monaghan is next for the Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺

The rolling drumlin landscape of Monaghan that so inspired Patrick Kavanagh is full of archaeology from all eras. Here's a few of my favourite sites:

1. Clones Round Tower
2. The Black Pig's Dyke
3. Lisnadarragh Wedge Tomb

The Kandariya Mahadeva Temple, meaning "the Great God of the Cave", is the largest and most ornate Hindu temple in the medieval temple group found at Khajuraho in Madhya Pradesh, India. It is considered one of the best examples of temples preserved from the medieval period in India.

Bramante’s Tempietto, door from inside.
Donato Bramante, Tempietto, c. 1502, San Pietro in Montorio, Rome. The “Tempietto” or little temple is a martyia (a building that commemorates a martyrdom) that marks the traditional site of Saint Peter’s crucifixion.

Picking up the Irish Archaeology Alphabet with County Louth 🏺 

Ireland's smallest county packs a big punch when it comes to heritage. It has the finest high cross in the country at Monasterboice, as well as one of the best castles at Castleroche. The Proleek Dolmen isn't bad either!

A wonderful snowy scene of the 6.6 metre tall menhir in the grounds of the Chateau Coadélan, Côtes-D'Armor. Happy Standing Stone Sunday, Christmas, Yule, belated Solstice etc everyone and thanks for following us! 🏺

It's County Waterford for tonight's Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺 

One of my favourite counties, it has wonderfully diverse landscapes from lush river valleys, rocky coastlines and monumental mountains. A few personal favourites: Gaulstown Dolmen, Nire Valley Standing Stone, Knockboy Medieval Church

A most unexpected sight in the baptistry in Cremona, Italy. The crocodile is suspended from the roof as an art installation. It gives the impression of being sucked into a portal to another world (in fact, the dome of the baptistry).

The Moundville Duck Bowl, a stunning example of Indigenous Mississippian culture and craft, carved from a single piece of stone. Discovered in the early 1900s in Alabama. Made in the 13th or 14th C. 📷 Moundville Archaeological Park

We're in County Laois for tonight's Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺 

Despite its relatively small size, Laois punches above its weight in terms of monuments. Here's a few favourites that come to mind:

1. The Rock of Dunamase (a 13th century castle)

Gift from Santa, the skills of the Medieval stone masons will always amaze & this perfect sun dial from 12thC Notre Dame du Lac in Le Thor, Provence, is a thing of great beauty. Younger & fitter stone mason apprentices were known for climbing to the very top of a church and carving VERY RUDE THINGS.

This is the starry mosaic firmament in the C5th CE mausoleum of Galla Placidia in Ravenna with symbols of the 4 evangelists in the corners. It’s terrific. There also some blokes in robes pointing. The other pic is the exterior. I’ve been lucky enough to visit Ravenna & if you get the chance, GO!

Very sexy folds developed in thinly interbedded limestones and shales of the Cretaceous Palombini shale formation at Acquarilli beach, Island of Elba.

This is one of the formations that was squeezed in an accretionary prism before the collision that generated the Northern Apennines.

This is the oldest statue in London. Carved in the 2nd century, this effigy of Minerva was rediscovered in the 19th century when the head of King Alfred was added of Coade stone, and  it was installed in the garden at Trinity Church Sq in 1826.

#ClassicsTober23 day 28 Nyx goddess of night not in #Pompeii b/c of that pre-Olympian problem. But (follow my thought process here) it's dark at night, Dark Side of the Moon oh! Pink Floyd played in the amphitheatre, which is one of the oldest in Italy ca. 70s BCE. 🏺🏛️

Thamugadi in Algeria was designed to be home to 10,000 retired legionaries, but soon outgrew its original allocation.Because of the remarkable state of preservation of the ruins & the unique example of Roman urban planning with clearly delineated grids, it was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site

As the foothold of the Normans, Wexford is a good place to discover medieval sites. Here's a few favourites: 

Hook Lighthouse, the ancient promontory fort at Baginbun that was the scene of a major medieval battle & Tintern Abbey

It's a year ago since I fulfilled a lifelong ambition to visit the Bronze Age site of Akrotiri on Santorini. It's a truly extraordinary place. Buildings destroyed in a volcanic eruption c. 1600 BC still stand, some up to 3 storeys high, many with their contents. 🏺

A series of camels carved into desert rock faces in north-western Saudi Arabia are actually prehistoric, dating from 7,000-8,000 years ago - before either the Pyramids of Giza or Stonehenge were built.

In addition to the oldest examples of basketry known in Europe, dating to 9500 BCE, la Cueva de los Murcielagos in Spain, is also home to the oldest known pair of shoes in Europe! (Photo Credit: MUTERMUR project). #Archaeology 🏺

A childlike and rather terrifying cupid pipes to Heracles in the House of Marcus Lucretius, Pompeii. 
The Latin name Cupīdō refers to earnest or passionate desire. Less romantic, the English word, cupidity, derives from the same root.
🏛 Naples Archaeological Museum 
🏺Ancientbluesky

Quite a thrill this week to see the ancient ruins at Akrotiri, on the island of Santorini. Remains of a once thriving and wealthy civilisation, destroyed when most of the island exploded in a volcanic eruption around 1600 BC. 

#AncientSiteSunday

This is a crackerjack example of ancient Egyptian glassware. This is an opaque, polychrome glass bottle in the shape of a ‘bulti’ fish. It dates from the 18th dynasty so it’s roughly mid-2nd millennium BCE. 

🏛️📷 British Museum

Stèle de Saint-Patrice monolith in the wall of a private garden, Lannion, Côtes-D'Armor. A large Christianized Gaulish stele which once held several statues of saints. Also called "menhir of justice", having probably served as a gallows. 🏺 More 1/

This fresco depicts Polyphemus and the nereid Galatea in a landscape. Galatea is sometimes also the name of the statue that Pygmalion creates, though these were likely different story traditions…

Enthralled by this stained glass from the cathedral at Le Mans showing the Virgin Mary whacking a man over the head with a hammer. 

Adams' "Mont-St-Michel & Chartres" says it depicts a miracle story told in the Maine about Mary, but I haven't been able to trace it further—anyone know? #medievalsky

Santa Maria del Fiore, Florence.  More commonly known as the Duomo. The largest dome built in 1000 years

Thought I'd be obsessed with that dome, but really I can't get over the fit and finish of the tri-color marble cladding. Its patterns are visually perfect. Impossible to overstate the skill here

Skalat (Ternopil): Wichrowski Castle, church, old buildings. One of the best-preserved castles in Ternopil is located in Skalat, a town of district subordination on the Hnyla River in the Pidvolochysk district of Ternopil region. ⬇

#ClassicsTober23 17: Atalanta

A powerful figure from Greek myth here depicted by Tyler Miles Lockett slaying two centaurs after they attempted to rape her. Atalanta was a devotee of Artemis and like the huntress herself sought a life of independence.

🏺ClassicsBluesky

Oldest-surviving anatomical theatre, located in Padua and built in 1594. Over the entrance is the Latin inscription: “hic est locus ubi mors gaudet succurrere vitam.” Roughly: “this is the place where death delights to help life (or the living).” 

Photo: Rolando Paolo Guerzoni.

For the 17th day of ClassicsTober (Atalanta), a sarcophagus depicting the Calydonian Boar Hunt. Meleager stands at center, and to his right is Atalanta with her bow, drawing an arrow from her quiver. Found in Vicovaro, now in the Musei Capitolini in Rome.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

Besides their role as tools or weapons, axes always had social or spiritual meaning - as markers of status or ritual paraphernalia. 
Gold double axe, one of 19 from the Arkalochori Cave, Crete, 1700-1600 BCE. Museum Heraklion. #Archaeology 🏺

ReliefWednesday; from the Museo Nacional de Arte Romano in Mérida, a banqueting scene related to Mithras, whose rock birth is depicted in a sculpture at the left of the scene. Found locally in Mérida at the Casa dei Mitreo.

Witnessing the vibrant colours of freshly uncovered frescoes in Pompeii is a privilege and joy that never fades.
Seeing the latest discovery of a mythological scene—Phrixus seated on a ram while his sister Helle drowns—in the context of the room it decorated is no exception. 🏺

Dendera Temple, Egypt. Relief depicts Cleopatra & her son with Julius Caesar Caesarion.
After Cleopatra’s suicide, Caesarion fled but Octavian called for his return. Arius Didymus reportedly said, ‘Too many Caesars are not good.’ Octavian had Caesarion (aged 17) killed in 30 BC.

🏺Ancientbluesky

Excavations underneath Basilica San Giovanni in Laterano (Rome) revealed vivid fresco in what were 2nd c. CE elite houses, followed by a complex sequence of spatial reuse and rebuilding ending up with the first ever cathedral. 
🏺 ClassicsBlueSky

Twelve Roman amphorae lost in a shipwreck off the coast of the Mediterranean island of Gozo 2100+ years ago; they then lay on the seabed until underwater archaeology brought them to the surface in 1961.

Longmen Grottoes in Henan Province, China. Over 2,300 caves filled with over 100,000 statues of all sizes. 4th-10th-centuries CE

This is a UNESCO-recognized World Heritage site, representing the pinnacle of Chinese stone carving art.

No better way to end my year of church crawling with the Jesse window from St Mary the Virgin, Shrewsbury, Shropshire. 

This C14 window was moved twice - was originally at the Greyfriars chapel along the Severn. How it survived the Reformation is unclear.

A beauty. 

🗃️#medievalsky

San Lorenzo, Turin 1668-1687, Italian Baroque masterpiece designed by Guarino Guarini, architect & mathematician, born  #OTD 1624; known for his use of complex geometry & innovative structural engineering techniques.

ClassicsTober23 Phaedra, a victim of the gods, was cursed by Aphrodite to fall in love with her stepson Hippolytus. Why? Because he devoted himself to Artemis & not love 🤷🏻‍♂️. It ends in shame & death for Phaedra, & Hippolytus mangled under a chariot. No wonder she looks haunted
🏛️📷BM ClassicsBlueSky

Wanapum flow of Columbia River Basalt entered a lake here, just south of I-84, a bit east of The Dalles on Rte 97. The transition from pillow basalt to columnar jointing ~indicates the lakes surface.⚒️ GeoHols for scale in left photo.

The trireme, a marvel of military ship building. Here are 2 ancient reliefs, the 1st from Rhodes carved into a cliff face (📷Saffron Blaze), the 2nd from Athens (📷Acropolis 🏛️). The other 2 pics are the ‘Olympias’, a modern reconstruction which an inexperienced crew got to 17kph!

Bologna's leaning tower: The Garisenda Tower in Bologna is not as famous as the Tower of Pisa, but it leans a little more. Lately, though, the dynamic of its movement has become worrisome, and city officials have closed a city-centre square as they study what to do about the tower.

The shaman of Bad Dürrenberg are the remains of a 25-35 year old woman, who was buried 8600 to 9000 year ago in Germany. Around her, were the remains of an extraordinary head-dress, made from the bones and teeth of different animals such as deer, wild boar, crane and turtle.

Currently on display at the Cluny Museum in Paris, this ancient spear head made from rock crystal was found in south west France and probably dates from the Ice Age. Although its shape is not unusual, the use of the crystal suggests it had special significance.

A really great post about how Romans did & didn't live. I'm going to use it to completely shamelessly promote an old video I did on several of Pompeii's homes -- from the grandest to the humblest. What "home" meant. 🏺 #AncientBlueSky

Pachar, Ollantaytambo, Peru

The Madrague de Giens is a Roman shipwreck off the coast of S France, sinking mid C1stBCE. It was a huge merchant ship, 40m in length. Fully laden it could hold 520 tons of cargo. The amphorae found held wine, oil & other goods like garum. Its discovery was also a breakthrough in marine archaeology

The  door at the foot of the tower of 14th century St Andrew’s Church, Kenn, Devon, framed by yews. The present church is built of local red sandstone with Beer stone mouldings, on the site of an earlier church.

Ancient Olympic Games, quadrennial athletic event that was held in Olympia, Greece, from 776 BCE to about 393 CE. It was part of a religious festival that honoured Zeus, and the name Olympics was derived from Mount Olympus, home of the Greek gods.

The reliquary of the jaw of St. Anthony of Padua (Basilica del Santo, Padua) is crazy enough as it is, but in the modern times they added two light sources, to illuminate it better. The lights reflect from a glass sphere that holds the jaw, looking like ghostly eyes.

(See the 3d photo for no-eyes)

Stunned no Arachne in Pompeii (not even in a fullery!), thought to hop to Rome (Forum Transitorium has the best frieze) but instead here's the other half of her myth: my fav Athena from the Villa of Papyri MANN inv. 6007. That feathered cape! #ClassicsTober23 day 13. 🏺

The world's oldest condom, made of pig intestines in 1640 CE, discovered in Lund, Sweden. The owners manual advised men to dip it in unboiled milk before use to prevent diseases when engaging with prostitutes. It’s washable and reusable. On display at Tirolean County Museum in Austria.

Most of the historic ceramics that I've copied have a surface design. 18th C. British agateware is different, made of two clays wedged together. During the trimming process the marbling is revealed. I love how this one turned out! Sherds were recovered in St. Augustine, Florida .
#maniarchaeology 🏺

Did you know that ancient Egypt had porn? (kinda) Turin Erotic Papyrus, ca 1150 BC, found at Deir el Medina (Luxor), shows 12 vignettes, the women are beautiful+ the men have facial hair+ bellies with enormous penises (sound familiar?). This was NOT normal for ancient Egypt.

The churchyard of St Nicholas Church, Kenilworth extends over the site of the Augustinian Priory of St Mary’s founded in 1119. 

The Norman doorway appears to have been reset, perhaps from the priory church.

A beautiful Roman glass bottle, made about 2000 years ago, probably as a perfume bottle. The bands of colour, particularly the turquoise-y blue, make this a lovely example of this type of ancient glass - I really want one!

Book bag under one arm, squirming Christ child under the other ~ carved by an unknown master from a single block of limestone, this is the 3/4 life-size polychrome Nostre Dame de Grasse, dating from late-15th century (photo: Nick Thompson; Musée des Augustins, Toulouse)

This simple yet beautiful jade-coloured glass bottle was found in a C2nd CE grave in St Albans. It’s a lovely little piece and incredible to think that it survived unbroken for the best part of 2,000 years in the earth. 

🏛️Verulanium Museum
📷 mine

Feeling volcanic today, so here's some #Fieldwork prettiness for you! 

These basalt columns are found at Devils Postpile near Mammoth Lakes, California. After ponded lava filled the San Joaquin River valley 80,000 years ago, it cooled toward the flow's center, shrank, and cracked into polygons. ⚒️🧪🌋

Yes, lots of people died at Pompeii and Herculaneum when Vesuvius erupted in A.D. 79 - but what about the far greater number of people who survived? Where did they go?

Fascinating archaeology article about how people moved on after major disasters in the ancient world:

Roman two-handled glass bottle ~ C1st CE

This exquisite bottle is translucent cobalt blue with handles matching in the same colour.

Looking closely at the body, you can see some bubbling in the glass as well as iridescence on the interior.

🏛The Met, 17.194.157

The cathedral of St Mary, Limerick, is also home to the only complete set of misericords to survive from medieval Ireland. These beautifully carved hinged oak seats, dating from the 15th century, were designed to give support to clergy who had to stand during long services.

If you see this, post a ship

The Roman Emperor Caligula had built two massive, elaborate palace-ships on Italy's Lake Nemi, the larger and more elaborate of which featured marble structures, mosaic floors, heating, plumbing, and a full Roman bath facility.

An interesting find! It looks very much like it's the burial ground of the Premonstratensian abbey of Woodburn, a late 12th century foundation. Woodburn had links to the Anglo-Norman lord John de Courcy, who was himself the builder of the nearby Carrickfergus Castle.

medievalsky 🗃️

A touch of ancient colour! Discovered in 1834 at the House of the Ancient Hunt, Pompeii (VII.4.48), this panel shows a gorgeously-attired Ariadne handing the skein of thread over to a blue-chlamys-clad Theseus.

Archaeologists in Germany have unearthed a sword from a Bronze Age burial, and the weapon is in such good condition that it still gleams. The 3,000-year-old sword, discovered in the town of Nördlingen in Bavaria, was found in the burial of a man, woman and child.

The large sardonyx Blacas Cameo, one of the Augustan ‘State Cameos’ from the imperial inner court. It depicts the ageless emperor from behind, turned to the left, wearing the aegis of Jupiter and Minerva. 

Made in Rome, ca. 14-20 CE.

Taken 21 Dec 2023 at the #BritishMuseum 🏺

Chartres cathedral, constructed within a generation after a fire in 1194, is rightly famous for its stained glass windows. But after visiting today I think the exterior carvings deserve equal adulation - truly incredible sources for material culture around 1200. #medievalsky

Agrigento today is a beautiful place - a hill town with gelateria, baroque churches & its famous temples, but it was also home to the tyrant Phalaris who roasted victims alive in a bronze bull. It’s said the contraption was made to amplify the screams of his victims

The Genoese built a series of towers and walls to fortify their colony at Galata (across the Golden Horn from Constantinople) in the 14th and 15th century. In addition to Galata Tower, much more survives than is commonly known.

This is the Temple of Apollo in Syracuse. It was constructed in the C6th BCE in the Doric style and is one of the first temples in Sicily with a peripteros (a colonnade of columns surrounding the inner sanctuary). Like many ruined classical temples, it’s the haunt of cats. 

📷 mine
🏺 AncientBlueSky

An iron dagger was found in King Tut's tomb but the Egyptian iron age was 250 years after his death.
Outside of Egypt there was a small amount of iron work but it was only used for small jewelry and ceremonial items. It was worth more than gold.
One theory is the dagger was made out of a meteor.

A Roman iron trident-head, found during excavations in central London. It may have been used by a gladiator who fought in the Londinium amphitheatre, which is just a mile away from the find site - if so, a retiarius gladiator, who fought with a trident & net AncientBlueSky

At first glance you might think that this alabaster tomb might be from Westminster Abbey but this remarkable monument to Alice Chaucer is at St. Mary, Ewelme in Oxfordshire. She was the grandaughter of Geoffrey Chaucer. Note the stone cadaver behind the tracery at the bottom.

This is the 14th-century pele tower in Corbridge, Northumberland. Built from recycled Roman stones, it was once the vicarage back in the days of constant Scottish raids. Nowadays it is an events venue with a gin bar on the ground floor.

The Golden Hat of Berlin is one of 4 found in Europe, all from the Bronze Age, and is also the best preserved hat among them.  These hats are actually astronomical calendars with which the seasons were calculated for the benefit of agricultural production. 🏺🏺🏺

10th century BC, Germany.

A murmuration of brick in Wickham, Hampshire of 1648. This has to be one of my favourite brick buildings. A glorious attempt at a polite frontage in a vernacular building is made all the more divine by its idiosyncrasies. Full of personality.

11: Pandora, pulchra percontatrix (beautiful and inquisitive), has opened the pyxis vetita (forbidden box) or dolium vetitum (forbidden jar), freeing a swarm of mala (evil things), to the satisfaction of Zeus.
🏺ClassicsTober with 🐭 Minimus

The Countdown to St. Patrick's Day.  This is the Giant's Causeway in County Antrim.   It's an area with 40,000 interlocking basalt columns, created over 50-million years ago. This is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.   The album cover for Led Zeppelin's "Houses of the Holy” was photographed here.

I love how people rave about minimalism and a classic look but it's just invented by racists in the 19th century to praise themselves for being boring. Anyways, heres ancient glass bowls from c. 2 C bce.

Ancient DNA is amazing, Part 4,982. DNA of 400-yr-old cow bones in Hispaniola and Mexico hints that the first cowboys in the Americas were enslaved Africans. Analysis backs up historians' belief that classic cowboy stuff like herding from horseback and using lassos were created by Africans, too.

Reliefs of battles, hunts, weddings, and legends on Roman sarcophagi are fine, but take a moment to admire the heads or masks carved at the corners (acroteria) of the lids. These are four of my favorites from the Capitoline Museums, Rome.

The famous monumental statue of the Mexica (Aztec) goddess Coatilcue was buried by the Spanish invaders near the site of the destroyed Templo Mayor. Rediscovered in 1790, Antonio León y Gama made these illustrations of it for his 1792 book on the object, before it was reburied.

This is absolutely magnificent and unique. 

It’s an ancient British bronze helmet and is the only Celtic horned helmet ever found. It was found in the Thames by Waterloo so was likely a votive offering deposited in the river. Originally, it would have gleamed like gold

County Tyrone is tonight's feature in the Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺 

Lots of very cool monuments here, a few favourites include the remarkable stone circles and alignments at Beaghmore, Tullaghoge Fort and the ogham stone at Aghascrebagh (who's placename means the 'Field of the Writing').

Machu Picchu is an ancient Inca citadel located high in the Andes Mountains of Peru. It was built in the 15th century and abandoned a hundred years later. Discovered by Hiram Bingham in 1911, it has become a popular tourist destination.

Hello, bluesky! I often share photos of beautiful things using the #SomethingBeautiful tag, usually things from Italy (where I do research). This is a wrought iron dragon bracket on the side of a Medieval building in Assisi. If you'd enjoy photos of history & beauty, you'll find them here!

This is a large portion of a salt glazed stoneware crock made by the company TO Goodwin, located in the Hartford, CT area - likely produced in the mid 1800s. Found over three field seasons. There are about 20 other fragments. #Archaeology 🧪🏺

Historian friends, help needed to identify this coat of arms. It's from a set of arms of powers discussed by Machiavelli, grouped w/ Holy Roman Emperor & kings of France, Naples, & Aragon plus Venice. Likely not an Italian family, they're a different section. Ideas?

The oldest-surviving anatomical theatre in the world, built in 1594, and located in Padua. At the center stands a table on which the dissections of human or animal bodies took place. You can still visit it today. Photo: Daily Art Magazine.

1/ Jan. 20: St Sebastian
In the former cemetery by San Secondo, Gubbio, Umbria, the Cappella Panfili has three paintings of St Sebastian’s martyrdom and burial and a vault decorated with the Doctors of the Church. Let’s take a look.
MedievalSky

Three ancient Roman shoes, all discovered at the fort of Trimontium in Scotland and now on display in the Trimontium Museum, Melrose. The Romans tanned leather by soaking it in tree bark and often decorated their shoes with fancy cut or stamped decoration.

Part of a colossal marble statue from the 1st century AD, probably of Jupiter, that once stood on Rome's Capitoline Hill. Today it's a commanding presence in the wonderful National Museum of Archaeology (MANN) in Napoli, where the modern god Maradona's flags fly in every street. 🏺#AncientBlueSky

In every one of Göbekli Tepe’s monumental buildings there’s at least one pillar which stands out by exceptionally rich decorations. Illustrations of Stone Age myths? Pillar 43, Building D. #Archaeology 🏺

This is the simply MAGNIFICENT early 13th century imperial crown of Constance of Aragon, who was wife of the Holy Roman Emperor, Frederick II, known as stupor mundi (or, wonder of the world). It’s housed in the Treasury of Palermo Cathedral.

The 11c St Sophia Cathedral in Kjiv inspired by Ag Sophia in Constantinople; much of the work was done by craftsmen from that city. Examples of the striking mosaics in the #Byzantine style: The Annunciation (the 2 figures are on adjacent columns); Church Fathers; the Eucharist; the High Priest Aaron

There is something very moving and touching when a face from the past emerges from the ash in Pompeii. And this is no exception: the blue sclera of the eyes are extraordinary. 
Newly discovered medallion with a portrait of a woman from a house on via del Vesuvio.

Wow, I’ve never seen this before. It’s a Bronze Age pendant (c.1,000BCE)  thought to represent the sun that was found only 2 years ago in Shropshire. The profusion of triangles are almost hypnotic especially when combined with those incised lines that add texture and depth.

El Infiernito (Spanish for "Little hell"), is a pre-Columbian Muisca site in Columbia! It is composed of several earthworks, standing stones and burial mounds are also present. The site was a center of religious ceremonies and probably also served as a rudimentary astronomical observatory. 🏺More 1/2

Agetria (Hodegetria) is a small domed Byzantine church built by cliffs on the coast of Inner Mani, a region in Southern Greece. The small peninsula of Tigani visible to the north was probably the site of an important Frankish castle in the 13th century.

The hominin skull from Steinheim, Germany is around 250,000 years old. It likely belongs to an early Neandertal population, but has been the subject of debate ever since it was uncovered in a gravel quarry in 1933.

The Rotunda of Thessaloniki is is usually identified as the Mausoleum of Galerius, who made Thessaloniki an imperial residence around 300, though it has also been attributed to Constantine. It was converted into a church between the late 4th and the early 6th century.

Carved from wood, this 16th-century figure shows a corpse inside a coffin. The body is decaying and worms are spilling from the stomach. The coffin is a memento mori, an object that reminds the viewer of the shortness of human life & the inevitability of death.

Fort Rock, a tuff ring about a mile in diameter, north of the town of the same name in central Oregon. Wave action in the pluvial lake here in the Pleistocene eroded it into the amphitheater shape we see today.⚒️

For FrescoFriday, from the triclinium of the House of Cornelius Diadumenus at Pompeii, a scene depicting the nymph Callisto (standing at left) and Artemis (seated with Eros). Now in the Museo Archeologico Nazionale di Napoli.

The Hungarian Rocks, or stones, are a group of tall vertical cliffs located in the middle of a spruce forest in the Carpathians.

#ClassicsTober23 day 18 from the triclinium of the Villa Arianna in Stabiae, Hippolytus introduces Phaedra (and the rest of the world) to the concept of talk to the hand. His face really didn't want to listen to his stepmother. 🏺

Paisley Abbey was first built in the 12th century. In 1991, it underwent some necessary restoration work as 12 of its 13 gargoyles were so badly ruined from water damage they had to be removed. One stone mason had a little fun & one gargoyle bears a strong resemblance to the Xenomorph from Alien

A stunning Roman bowl made of vivid blue glass. It was found in Nijmegen (Netherlands) in absolutely perfect condition - without even the slightest fracture or chip - despite being around 2000 years ago. An absolutely amazing find! 🏺AncientBlueSky

The Iron Crown of Lombardy was made in the Middle Ages. Legend maintains its central band was beaten from a nail of the True Cross. It was used in the coronation of the first Holy Roman Emperor Charlemagne on Christmas day 800 and was worn again in 1805 by Napoleon when he was crowned King of Italy.

For ClassicsTober day 3; part of a statue depicting Asterion, the Minotaur. A 1st century CE Roman copy of a group with Theseus dating to the 5th century BCE, possibly sculpted by Myron. Found in Rome and now in the Museo Nazionale Romano, Palazzo Massimo alle Terme.

Standing outside the mouth of Arma dello Stefanin, July 2022. This site houses a long sequence of Upper Paleolithic, Mesolithic and Neolithic levels. Originally excavated in the 50s and 60s and then again in the 80s, my team and I have been working there for the past two years 🏺🧪

Belt buckles and bracelets made from Spondylus shells, a species living in the Mediterranean / Black Sea in depths around 15m, were between the favorite ornaments of the Linear Pottery Culture in Central Europe between 5500-4800 BCE. Historisches Museum Regensburg. #Archaeology 🏺

Wood is rarely preserved in archaeological contexts. A Late Bronze Age grave from Behringersdorf in southern Germany is an exception. It has parts of a wooden scabbard and of a quiver with bronze arrows. NHM Nürnberg #archaeology 🏺

Extensive research found a dismantled stone circle in West Wales that was moved to the current site of Stonehenge thousands of years ago.

On February 17, 1600, philosopher, cosmologist, and unrepentant heretic Giordano Bruno was burned at the stake in the Campo de' Fiori in Rome. A notary sketched the execution as it happened. In 1889, a statue of Bruno by Ettore Ferrari was unveiled in the Campo, where it stands today #earlymodern

St. Kilmacrehy Church, Lahinch, Ireland.
Records start during the 14th century for this place, with a mysterious bone stealing monster (more than likely an otter or somesuch) carving hidden away near the back. This is why most of the graves here have slabs covering them.

The Löwenmensch figurine, also called the Lion-man of Hohlenstein-Stadel, is a prehistoric ivory sculpture discovered in Hohlenstein-Stadel, a German cave, in 1939. Determined by carbon dating to be 35,000-41,000 years old, it is one of (if not *the*) oldest anthropomorphic art pieces.

Holy moly! This looks like some glorious Ottoman helmet, but no, it’s Iron Age Celtic.

Statue of Vercingetorix, Alesia, France. 
 
Caesar’s noblest Gallic enemy surrendered himself to spare his men following the Battle of Alesia in 52 BC. Caesar himself lauded his opponent in his Commentaries and Plutarch also poignantly narrated the account in his Life of Caesar.

Around 2,200 years ago, the artist Hephaistion left his signature on a mosaic floor in the form of a piece of parchment held down with small drops of wax. This perfect imitation of a sheet of parchment, with one corner curled up, was discovered in one of the palaces at Pergamon.

#TombTuesday - The Lion Tomb in the Qasr al-Bint Nabatean Necropolis of Hegra (now in Saudi Arabia). The tomb is decorated with a carving of two felines on either side of a rosette. The name of the stonemason who carved the tomb, Aftah, is engraved above the left feline.

Archaeology often evokes digging, but (re)studying collections curated in museums is also a key part of the job. This past May/June, at the Museum of Albenga, our team began reanalyzing stone tools from Arma dello Stefanin made by some of the last hunter-gatherers in NW Italy ca. 10k years ago. 🧪

I just love this manuscript. European Columbines and Sweet Cherry, from Mira Calligraphiae Monumenta (a calligraphy manual!), made in Vienna by Georg Bocskay (text, 1562) and Joris Hoefnagel (illumination c. 1591-1596). 
At the Getty Museum, Ms. 20 (86.MV.527), fol. 28.

Some clay pipe bowl loveliness for you this morning. Getting harder to find complete clay pipes in the Thames, they *do* wash up but more likely you’ll find just the bowl or perhaps a partial stem with a makers’ name. This is a beauty; 19thC pipe bowl in acorn design with a tiny acorn at the heel.

This is the oldest preserved human brain 🧠 at 2600 yrs old, found in Heslington, Yorkshire, UK in 2008.

The original owner was hanged, decapitated & the head immediately buried in mud.  Presumed ritual murder/sacrifice from the British Iron Age.

It's shrunk to 20% its original volume.

The Mosaic of Bacchus, discovered in 2009 in Saint-Laurent-d'Agny near Lyon, is thought to be the oldest known representation of the god of wine on a mosaic in Gaul. It was created around AD 10-14 and decorated the triclinium floor of a Roman domus. Bacchus is crowned with ivy and holding a thyrsus.

Portrait bust of the Roman emperor Trajan ~ c. 108 CE

Often lauded as the ‘optimus princeps’, his reputation as being the best since Augustus 👀 can be attributed in part to Pliny the Younger’s effusive Panegyricus! 

🏛 Kunsthistorisches Museum, Vienna

A marble carpet in Siena's Duomo, created over the course of six centuries. Two thirds remains covered with special panels that protect it. You can admire the uncovered floor every year from 18 August until the end of October. Here: a detail of Matteo di Giovanni's Massacre of the Innocents.

Icon of the Archangel Michael

for the mosque pic fans, here are my four best from the Rüstem Paşa mosque, which was made even more beautiful because it was hidden amid the most aggressive, multilingually yelling, and crowded market you can imagine. part of my spirit is still sitting here.

Day 14 of Classicstober (Helen): from the House of Jason at Pompeii, a wall painting depicting Cupid/Eros bringing Helen and Paris together. Dated to the 1st century CE. Now in the Museo Archeologico Nazionale di Napoli.

#RuinoftheDay -The Mithraeum House in Augusta Emerita (Mérida, Spain), built around three collonaded courts with an interior garden and a central pool. The quality of the floor mosaics is very high, the most fascinating one being the cosmological mosaic that once covered the floor of the triclinium.

Chepstow Castle, Monmouthshire is the oldest surviving post-Roman stone fortification in Britain. Located above cliffs on the River Wye, construction began in 1067 under the instruction of the Norman Lord William FitzOsbern. 

It was the southernmost of a chain of castles built in the Welsh Marches.

Chiricahua Nat. Monument (SE Arizona) has fabulous landscapes, views, and especially stone spires, pinnacles, and balanced and tumbled rocks, shaped by erosion after a cataclysmic volcanic eruption 27 million years ago covered the area with ash that hardened into rock. Gets remarkably few visitors!

An architectural and historical monument of the XIII-XVIII centuries, located in the village of Olesko, Zolochiv district, Lviv region. One of the oldest castles in Ukraine, built on the plateau of a hill that rises among the floodplain of the Libertsia River.

A mummy portrait from Roman Egypt, depicting a young woman who lived & died just under 2000 years ago, shown wearing some beautiful jewellery. It is always amazing to see the face of an ordinary (if probably quite wealthy) person from the ancient world (Photo: Getty Museum) AncientBlueSky

A beautiful & colourful Roman glass bowl, with a very eye-catching flower-y design in bold yellow, lilac, white & light green. It was made around 2000 years ago - but certainly does not look its age! (📷 Thorvaldsens Museum)

The dramatic Nightingale monument by Roubiliac at Westminster Abbey. Joseph Nightingale tries in vain to protect his wife as death emerges from the locked vault. Death is partially shrouded and stares up at the pair. 🗃️ #c18th

Who wants to help me with some Gothic Revival iconography? I'm helping a local church identify some windows and I can't place this guy. Clues are (1) Episcopal/Anglican church; (2) English-born patrons; (3) In memory of a surgeon; (4) Made between 1927 and 1931.

Things I absolutely love about the cover of The Borgia Dove: the keys and sun in the rondels over Giulia's head are the keys and sun from the ceiling of the Borgia Apartments in the Vatican! @@243251 has beautifully echoed Pinturicchio's work and it's gorgeous!

This is the Ashmolean Parchment, one of the oldest known love spells. Written in Coptic around 600 CE likely from Egypt, it binds Phello, son of Maure, to seek out and satisfy the desires of the caster, also listed in the text. Who cast it? Apapolo, son of Nooe.

Queer Pining: As old as time itself

The same builders that produced the Vikings’ boats can be credited for this Stavkirke (or "Stave Church") in Borgund, Norway. Constructed in 1180. See the actual, really cool, church in the next post.

Copper issue of Shahrwaraz, the Sasanian general & governor of Egypt after 617. Based on the issues of Maurice, it shows the “emperor” surrounded by the Sasanian Sun & Crescent emblem. Mint mark ΑΛΕΞ on the reverse. 

Sasanians ruled in Egypt at least until 628, but probably as late as 630.

Though I have yet to visit it, the porta nigra in modern Trier is one of my favourite Roman sites. It’s a wonderful (substantial) ruin and this early 20th century postcard of the gate makes it look even better. I love the sightseers at the bottom in their very best clothes.

The Great Barn at Harmondsworth is the largest standing medieval timber-framed structure in Britain.

Built in 1425, it's regarded as an outstanding example of medieval carpentry, built with the same skill and attention to detail as palaces.

I was in Abernethy last week to see @@504790 give a talk, which gave me the chance to visit and climb the town's amazing early medieval round tower. Built around 1100, it is one of only two in Scotland, although there are several in Ireland. Hard work, but worth it for the view!

Kastamonu (historic Kastamon) in Paphlagonia, associated with the Komnenian dynasty, was where Isaac I Komnenos was proclaimed emperor in 1057. Hüsamettin Çoban, a Seljuk (Turkish) commander, conquered it in the late 13th century.

This in Malmesbury (Wilts) is apparently the oldest inhabited building in England; converted into a house in the 1600s, it was originally the Saxon St Helen's Chapel, constructed around 900 AD. The large corner quoin (bottom right) is probably recycled from a Roman building.

The cathedral of Syracuse, Sicily, is a typically wild, ornate baroque extravaganza but inside, & built into its very fabric, are the remains of the vast Doric Temple of Athena built by Gelon after defeating the Carthaginians at Himera in 480BCE. Magnificent

The legacies of Roman Emperors can be varied. Vespasian, known for the Colosseum, is also more humbly associated with public toilets in Italy and France, known as Vespasiano and Vespasienne. This association arose as a result of a tax he imposed upon the collection of urine.

Another newly discovered medallion with a beautiful portrait of a woman. She too has those unusual blue-grey sclera and I love the “festive” wreath in her hair. 

From the House of Leda and the swan, Pompeii 🏺

This is believed to be the oldest depiction of Jesus.

The 'Alexamenos graffito' (singular of graffiti), is trolling a Roman soldier named Alexamenos, apparently a Christian, likely around 200 AD.

A man is shown worshiping a donkey-headed god on a crucifix.

Text: "Alexamenos worships [his] God"

A self-reminder that Corleck Head exists: a three-faced stone idol from Corleck Hill, County Cavan, Ireland. About 2000 years old; unearthed some time in 19 century, but kept as a farm gate decoration for decades (which de-contextualized the artifact). Related to the so-called "Celtic head cult"

Midnight memento mori jewellery - A white enamel skull stares out from the swivelling plate on this gold signet ring. The reverse features a merchant's mark and monogram. Around the plate is the message Mors Bonis Grata - 'Death is pleasing to the good' 16th cent. 🗃️ #earlymodern

For EpigraphyTuesday: the funerary inscription of Julia Peiris, an obstetrix (midwife) who 'did no harm.' Notably, the first T of obstetrix is shoehorned in between the S and E. Dated to the 1st century CE, from Trier. Now in the Rheinisches Landesmuseum Trier.

What a great week in Herefordshire. I visited to Kilpeck church - at last - and then Arthur's Stone. Lots more places on my bucket list, but these did very nicely in the mood eerie and ancient for now.

A small but charming bronze Roman statuette of Pegasus. It was found in an auxiliary cavalry fort in Dormagen (Germany) - was the winged horse a symbol for the unit stationed there, or perhaps adopted as an unofficial mascot?

I love these glimpses of everyday life from 2,000 years ago. It's a foreign country sometimes, the past, but then you see something that make you realise how similar we are: here, a dad buys a loaf of bread and his son clamours for it

County Meath is next in the Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺 

The Boyne Valley is ridiculously full of archaeology. Most associated with the great passage tombs of Newgrange, Knowth, and Dowth, but it has significant sites representing almost every era.

A few favourites; Newgrange, Tara and Loughcrew.

And another great discovery: archaeologists have unearthed a 6000- year-old Neolithic cursus- a ritual space - on the Isle of Arran, the only complete one to be found in the UK.

It stands close to the stone circles of Machrie Moor, an important ceremonial site.

Using paleoproteomics (ZooMS), my team identified a tiny hominin skull fragment at Riparo Bombrini (Level M4), in the Balzi Rossi site complex in NW Italy. Its age and cultural association material of the layer (43.5k cal. years BP, Mousterian tools) mean it's very likely a Neanderthal. 🏺🧪

This is statue of Saint Bartholomew, an early Christian martyr who was allegedly skinned alive. Notice: that's not a robe that he’s holding. It's his dissected skin. This stunning statute is by the Italian sculptor Marco d’Agrate, c.1562.

This splendid Greek bronze panoply is from the mid-C4th BCE and was made in southern Italy. The muscled or anatomical cuirass is particularly splendid and even includes nipples and navel. I can’t say it was modelled on my torso sadly. 

🏛️Rijksmeuseum van Oudeheden
📷Alexander van Loon

Temple of Vesta (Aedes Vestae) housed the sacred fire sometimes called Iliaci foci (hearth of Ilium). 

Ovid referred to Vesta in his Fasti vi. 265 as Vesta Iliaca. There was a strong connection between the myth of Rome’s Trojan origin and the flame.

🏺Ancientbluesky

Agrippina the Elder, mother of Caligula. 
Born in Athens, she hailed from the Julio-Claudian dynasty. 
She dressed her son in traditional soldier’s attire including the caliga from which he would derive his name and be known to the annals of history: Caligula.
🏛 Capitoline Museums
🏺Ancientbluesky

One of my favourite of Shropshire’s hidden gems is the 'Bayeux Tapestry of England'. In All Saints Church, Claverley. Spanning more than 15 metres in length, these lovely paintings date from the 13th century. The knights seem to leap to life. I'd definitely recommend a visit ✨️

Detail of Patroclus & Briseus as she is being taken away.
Patroclus had promised her she would wed Achilles, but when she returns, Patroclus has already been slain. She & Achilles are both left to lament him.
The House of the Tragic Poet in Pompeii contains many Iliadic scenes.

Love this intricate Hiberno-Romanesque decoration at Kilmore Cathedral, Cavan. The doorway (now incorporated in the 19th century cathedral) is thought to have originally come from the church on Trinity Island.

This relief, part of the Sebasteion at Aphrodisias is thought to depict a personification of the Roman emperor crowned by a personification of the Roman people (or the Senate or both!). The corona civica was awarded for saving the life of a citizen.

This 1,700-year-old blue glass bowl, a little more than 8 inches in diameter, was found buried next to a woman at the center of a cemetery at the Roman settlement of Emona in present-day Ljubljana in Slovenia.  Its Greek inscription reads: "Drink to live forever, for many years!"

The beautiful shrine of St Amphibalus at St Albans. If you look carefully, you can see a tiny man in a mask among the heads, added when the stonework was restored in 2020. Such a wonderful reflection of the ongoing life of the cathedral.

Stacked Roman amphora fragments at Monte Testaccio in #Rome, essentially an ancient dumping ground for unwanted vessels (reusing not being a notable phenomenon) - these are just a small number of up to 53 MILLION vessels abandoned here in antiquity!

The Roman wall surrounding the city of Lugo in Galicia is the only complete Roman wall remaining. According to legend the Romans built it to protect a forest and not a city, the“Sacred Forest of Augusto”. The walls still contain 85 towers and have survived intact for 18 centuries.
ancientbluesky 🏺

Yesterday we visited the remarkable St Mary's Church in Grandtully, Perthshire. The painted ceiling, centred on a representation of Death dates to c.1636, in a period when Charles I was encouraging the reintroduction of decoration into Scottish Churches.

Replica Bronze Age alderwood anthropomorphic figures at UCD Centre for Experimental Archaeology & Material Culture, an experiment tracing their deterioration over time, and comparing with originals found in peat bogs. Shows were not exposed to weather for more than months, before deposition in bogs.

Thonis-Heraklion was founded in C8thBCE at the Nile’s mouth. Before Alexandria’s creation, it was the major port of the delta. Succeeding earthquakes & floods saw the city slip under the waves in C2ndBCE eventually disappearing c.900CE. It has been excavated since 1933
📷Franck Goddio🏺 AncientBlueSky

Fantastic outcrop of the Oberalm Fm. with marls and clay-rich beds (background sedimentation), and calcareous turbitites. 
The iconic peaks of the Barmsteine - landmark of my hometown -  were formed due to the resistance of the thick, almost vertical turbitite beds to erosive surface processes.

The Antinous Mondragone is an extraordinary, well-preserved colossal head of Antinous that is believed to have been part of an acrolithic cult statue, a composite sculpture made of stone and wood.

Late Bronze Age baby-feeding vessels from Vösendorf, Austria, about 3000 years old. They have a tiny hole in the tail, so you can fill them with milk, and then an infant would hold it, and suck the milk through the tail. Ingenious and so pretty! Photo: Enver-Hirsch / Wien Museum

#ZigHealthTip If you're trying to lose weight (or gain it), do your best to weigh yourself daily. Why? Because your bodyweight can fluctuate by as much as FIFTEEN POUNDS from water retention. Daily weigh-ins allow you to extract averages. Log the weight, plot it on a graph and watch the trend.
